n250011: sn Chapters 1-4 are arranged in alphabetic-acrostic structures; the acrostic pattern does not appear in chapter 5. Each of the 22 verses in chapters 1, 2 and 4 begins with a successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet, while the acrostic appears in triplicate in the 66 verses in chapter 3. The acrostic pattern does not appear in chapter 5, but its influence is felt in that it has 22 verses, the same as the number of letters in the Hebrew alphabet. For further study on Hebrew acrostics, see W. M. Soll, вАЬBabylonian and Biblical Acrostics,вАЭ Bib 69 (1988): 305-23; D. N. Freedman, вАЬAcrostic Poems in the Hebrew Bible: Alphabetic and Otherwise,вАЭ CBQ 48 (1986): 408-31; B. Johnson, вАЬForm and Message in Lamentations,вАЭ ZAW 97 (1985): 58-73; K. C. Hanson, вАЬAlphabetic Acrostics: A Form Critical Study,вАЭ Ph.D. diss., Claremont Graduate School, 1984; S. Bergler, вАЬThreni VвАФNur ein alphabetisierendes Lied? Versuch einer Deutung,вАЭ VT 27 (1977): 304-22; E. M. Schramm, вАЬPoetic Patterning in Biblical Hebrew,вАЭ Michigan Oriental Studies in Honor of George S. Cameron, 175-78; D. N. Freedman, вАЬAcrostics and Metrics in Hebrew Poetry,вАЭ HTR 65 (1972): 367-92; N. K. Gottwald, вАЬThe Acrostic Form,вАЭ Studies in the Book of Lamentations, 23-32; P. A. Munch, вАЬDie alphabetische Akrostichie in der judischen Psalmendicthung,вАЭ ZDMG 90 (1936): 703-10; M. L√ґhr, вАЬAlphabetische und alphabetisierende Lieder im AT,вАЭ ZAW 25 (1905): 173-98.
n250012: tc The LXX and Vulgate (dependent on the LXX) include a preface that is lacking in the MT: вАЬAnd it came to pass after Israel had been taken captive and Jerusalem had been laid waste, Jeremiah sat weeping and lamented this lament over Jerusalem, and saidвА¶.вАЭ Scholars generally view the preface in the LXX and Vulgate as a later addition, though the style is Hebrew rather than Greek.
n250013:
tn The adverb вАП„Р÷µ„Щ„Ы÷Є„ФвАО ( Њekhah) is used as an exclamation of lament or desperation: вАЬHow!вАЭ (BDB 32 s.v.) or вАЬAlas!вАЭ (HALOT 40 s.v. 1.e). It is often the first word in laments (Isa 1:21; Jer 48:17; Lam 1:1; 2:1; 4:1, 2). Like the less emphatic exclamation вАП„Р÷µ„Щ„Ъ÷∞вАО ( Њekh, вАЬAlas!вАЭ) (2 Sam 1:19; Isa 14:4, 12; Ezek 26:17), it is used in contexts of lament and mourning.
sn The term вАП„Р÷µ„Щ„Ы÷Є„ФвАО ( Њekhah, вАЬAlas!вАЭ) and counterpart вАП„Р÷µ„Щ„Ъ÷∞вАО ( Њekh, вАЬAlas!вАЭ) are normally uttered in contexts of mourning as exclamations of lament over a deceased person (2 Sam 1:19; Isa 14:4, 12). The prophets borrow this term from its normal Sitz im Leben in the funeral lament and rhetorically place it in the context of announcements or descriptions of GodвАЩs judgment (Isa 1:21; Jer 48:17; Ezek 26:17; Lam 1:1; 2:1; 4:1, 2). This creates a personification of the city/nation that is either in danger of imminent вАЬdeathвАЭ or already has вАЬdiedвАЭ as a result of the LordвАЩs judgment.
n250014:
tn Heb вАЬgreat of people.вАЭ The construct вАП„®÷Ј„С÷Љ÷Є„™÷і„Щ „Ґ÷Є„ЭвАО (rabbati  њam, вАЬgreat of peopleвАЭ) is an idiom for large population: вАЬfull of people, populousвАЭ (BDB 912-13 s.v. I вАП„®÷Ј„СвАО; HALOT 1172 s.v. 7.a). The hireq-campaginis ending on вАП„®÷Ј„С÷Љ÷Є„™÷і„ЩвАО (rabbati) sometimes appears on construct forms (GKC 253 ¬І90.a,l). By contrast to the first half of the line, it is understood that she was full of people formerly. вАП„®÷Ј„С÷Љ÷Є„™÷і„Щ „Ґ÷Є„ЭвАО (rabbati  њam) may also be construed as a title.
sn Two thirds of Lamentations is comprised of enjambed lines (the syntax of a line carries on to the next line without a pause) rather than Hebrew poetryвАЩs more frequent couplets of parallel phrasing. This serves a rhetorical effect not necessarily apparent if translated in the word order of English prose. Together with the alphabetic acrostic form, these pull the reader/hearer along through the various juxtaposed pictures of horror and grief. For further study on the import of these stylistic features to the function of Lamentations see F. W. Dobbs-Allsopp, Lamentations (IBC), 12-20; idem, вАЬThe Enjambing Line in Lamentations: A Taxonomy (Part 1),вАЭ ZAW 113/2 (2001): 219-39; idem, вАЬThe Effects of Enjambment in Lamentations,вАЭ ZAW 113/5 (2001): 1-16. However, for the sake of English style and clarity, the translation does not necessarily reflect the Hebrew style and word order.
n250015: tn The noun вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„У÷Є„УвАО (badad, вАЬisolation, aloneвАЭ) functions as adverbial accusative of state. After verbs of dwelling, it pictures someone sitting apart, which may be linked to dwelling securely, especially of a city or people (Num 23:9; Deut 33:28; Jer 49:31; Ps 4:8 [9 HT]), or to isolation (Lev 13:46; Jer 15:17; 49:31). Applied to personified Jerusalem, it contrasts a possible connotation of dwelling securely, instead stating that Lady Jerusalem is abandoned and connoting that the city is deserted.
n250016: tn Heb вАЬgreat.вАЭ The adjective вАП„®÷Ј„СвАО (rav, вАЬgreatвАЭ) is used in reference to a position of prominence, leadership (Ps 48:3; Dan 11:3, 5) or strength (Isa 53:12; 63:1; 2 Chr 14:10) (BDB 913 s.v. 2.b; HALOT 1172 s.v. 6). The hireq-campaginis ending on вАП„®÷Ј„С÷Љ÷Є„™÷і„ЩвАО (rabbati) sometimes appears on construct forms (GKC 253 ¬І90.a,l). This adjective is the same word mentioned at the beginning of the verse in the phrase вАЬfull of people.вАЭ These may also be construed as epithets.
n250017: tn The kaf (вАП„ЫвАО) prefixed to вАП„Р÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ю÷Є„†÷Є„ФвАО ( Њalmanah, вАЬwidowвАЭ) expresses identity (вАЬhas become a widowвАЭ) rather than comparison (вАЬhas become like a widowвАЭ) (see HALOT 453 s.v. 1; BDB 454 s.v. вАП„Ы÷Љ÷∞вАО 1.d). The construction emphasizes the class of widowhood.
n250018:
tn The noun вАП„©„В÷Є„®÷Є„™÷і„ЩвАО (sarati, вАЬprincessвАЭ) is in construct with the following noun. The hireq-campaginis ending sometimes appears on construct forms (GKC 253 ¬І90.a,l).
sn Judah was organized into administrative districts or provinces under the rule of provincial governors (вАП„©„В÷Є„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО, sarim) (1 Kgs 20:14, 17, 19). The feminine term вАП„©„В÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (sarah, вАЬprincess, provincial governessвАЭ) is a wordplay alluding to this political background: personified Jerusalem had ruled over the Judean provinces.
n250019: tn Heb вАЬprincess among the provinces.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ю÷∞„У÷і„Щ„†÷Є„ФвАО (medinah) is an Aramaic loanword which refers to an administrative district or province in the empire (e.g., Ezek 19:8; Dan 8:2) (BDB 193 s.v. 2; HALOT 549 s.v.).
n2500110: tn Following the verb вАП„Ф÷Є„Щ÷Є„ФвАО (hayah, вАЬto beвАЭ), the preposition вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) designates a transition into a new state or condition: вАЬto becomeвАЭ (BDB 512 s.v. вАП„Ь÷∞вАО 4.a; e.g., Gen 2:7; 1 Sam 9:16; 15:1).
n2500111: tn The noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„°вАО (mas) means вАЬforced labor, corve√© slave, conscripted worker.вАЭ It refers to a subjugated population, subject to forced labor and/or heavy taxes (Gen 49:15; Exod 1:11; Deut 20:11; Josh 16:10; 17:13; Judg 1:28, 30, 33, 35; 1 Kgs 5:14 [5:28 HT]; 9:15, 21; 12:18; 2 Chr 10:18; Isa 31:8; Lam 1:1).
n2500112: tn Heb вАЬher tears are on her cheek.вАЭ
n2500113: tn Heb вАЬlovers.вАЭ The term вАЬloversвАЭ is a figurative expression (hypocatastasis), comparing JerusalemвАЩs false gods and foreign political alliances to sexually immoral lovers. Hosea uses similar imagery (Hos 2:5, 7, 10, 13). It may also function as a double entendre, first evoking a disconcerting picture of a funeral where the widow has no loved ones present to comfort her. God also does not appear to be present to comfort Jerusalem and will later be called her enemy. The imagery in Lamentations frequently capitalizes on changing the readerвАЩs expectations midstream.
n2500114: tn Heb вАЬJudah.вАЭ The term вАЬJudahвАЭ is a synecdoche of nation (= Judah) for the inhabitants of the nation (= people).
n2500115: tn There is a debate over the function of the preposition вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min): (1) a temporal sense of вАЬafterвАЭ (HALOT 598 s.v. 2.c; BDB 581 s.v. 4.b) (e.g., Gen 4:3; 38:24; Josh 23:1; Judg 11:4; 14:8; Isa 24:22; Ezek 38:8; Hos 6:2) is adopted by one translation: вАЬAfter affliction and harsh labor, Judah has gone into exileвАЭ (NIV); (2) a causal sense of вАЬbecauseвАЭ (HALOT 598 s.v. 6; BDB 580 s.v. 2.f) (e.g., Isa 5:13) is adopted by many English versions: вАЬJudah has gone into exile because of misery and harsh oppression/servitudeвАЭ (cf. KJV, NKJV, RSV, NRSV, NJPS); and (3) an instrumental sense of вАЬby, throughвАЭ is possible (BDB 579 s.v. 2.e): вАЬJudah has gone into exile under affliction, and under harsh servitudeвАЭ (NASB). The issue here is whether this verse states that Judah went into exile after suffering a long period of trouble and toil, or that Judah went into exile because of the misery and affliction that the populace suffered under the hands of the Babylonians. For fuller treatment of this difficult syntactical problem, see D. R. Hillers, Lamentations (AB), 6-7.
n2500116: tn Heb вАЬgreat servitude.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ґ÷≤„С÷є„У÷Є„ФвАО ( њavodah, вАЬservitudeвАЭ) refers to the enforced labor and suffering inflicted upon conquered peoples who are subjugated into slavery (Exod 1:14; 2:23; 5:9, 11; 6:9; Deut 26:6; 1 Kgs 12:4; 1 Chr 26:30; 2 Chr 10:4; 12:8; Isa 14:3; Lam 1:3).
n2500117: tn The antecedent of вАЬsheвАЭ is вАЬJudah,вАЭ which functions as a synecdoche of nation (= Judah) for the inhabitants of the nation (= people). Thus, вАЬsheвАЭ (= Judah) is tantamount to вАЬtheyвАЭ (= former inhabitants of Judah).
n2500118: tn The preposition вАП„С÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (bin) is used in reference to a location: вАЬbetweenвАЭ (BDB 107 s.v. 1).
n2500119: tn Heb вАЬdistresses.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ю÷µ„¶÷Ј„®вАО (metsar, вАЬdistressвАЭ) occurs only here and in Ps 118:5 (NIV вАЬanguishвАЭ). Here, the plural form вАП„Ю÷∞„¶÷Є„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (metsarim, lit., вАЬdistressesвАЭ) is an example of the plural of intensity: вАЬintense distress.вАЭ The phrase вАП„С÷Љ÷і„Щ„Я „Ф÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷∞„¶÷Є„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (bin hammetsarim, вАЬbetween the narrow placesвАЭ) is unparalleled elsewhere in the Hebrew scriptures; however, this line is paraphrased in вАЬThe Thanksgiving PsalmвАЭ from Qumran (Hodayoth = 1QH v 29), which adds the phrase вАЬso I could not get away.вАЭ Following the interpretation of this line at Qumran, it describes a futile attempt to flee from the enemies in narrow straits that thwarted a successful escape.
n2500120:
tn Heb вАЬroads of Zion.вАЭ The noun вАП„¶÷і„Щ÷Љ„Х÷є„ЯвАО (tsiyyon, Zion) is a genitive of direction (termination) following the construct noun, meaning вАЬroads to Zion.вАЭ
sn The noun вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„®÷∞„Ы÷µ„ЩвАО (darkhe, вАЬroadsвАЭ) is normally masculine in gender, but here it is feminine (e.g., Exod 18:20) (BDB 202 s.v.), as indicated by the following feminine adjective вАП„Р÷≤„С÷Љ÷µ„Ь÷Љ„Х÷є„™вАО ( Њavelot, вАЬmourningвАЭ). This rare feminine usage is probably due to the personification of Jerusalem as a bereaved woman throughout chap. 1.
n2500121:
tn The adjective вАП„Р÷≤„С÷Љ÷µ„Ь÷Љ„Х÷є„™вАО ( Њavelot, вАЬmourningвАЭ) functions as a predicate of state.
sn The term вАП„Р÷Є„С÷Љ÷Ј„ЬвАО ( Њaval, вАЬmournвАЭ) refers to the mourning rites for the dead or to those mourning the deceased (Gen 37:35; Job 29:25; Ps 35:14; Jer 16:7; Esth 6:12; Sir 7:34; 48:24). The prophets often use it figuratively to personify Jerusalem as a mourner, lamenting her deceased and exiled citizens (Isa 57:18; 61:2, 3) (BDB 5 s.v.; HALOT 7 s.v.).
n2500122: tn Heb вАЬfrom lack of.вАЭ The construction вАП„Ю÷і„С÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷і„ЩвАО (mibbeli) is composed of the preposition вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min), functioning in a causal sense (BDB 580 s.v. вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО 2.f), and the adverb of negation вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷і„ЩвАО (beli) to denote the negative cause: вАЬfrom want ofвАЭ or вАЬwithoutвАЭ (HALOT 133 s.v. вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷і„ЩвАО 4; BDB 115 s.v. вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷і„ЩвАО 2.c) (Num 14:16; Deut 9:28; 28:55; Eccl 3:11; Isa 5:13; Jer 2:15; 9:11; Hos 4:6; Ezek 34:5).
n2500123: tn Heb вАЬthose coming of feast.вАЭ The construct chain вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Р÷µ„Щ „Ю„Х÷є„Ґ÷µ„УвАО (ba Њe mo њed) consists of (1) the substantival plural construct participle вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Р÷µ„ЩвАО (ba Њe, вАЬthose who comeвАЭ) and (2) the collective singular genitive of purpose вАП„Ю„Х÷є„Ґ÷µ„УвАО (mo њed, вАЬfor the feastsвАЭ).
n2500124: tc The MT reads вАП„©„Б÷∞„Ґ÷Є„®÷ґ„Щ„Ф÷ЄвАО (she њareha, вАЬher gatesвАЭ). The BHS editors suggest revocalizing the text to the participle вАП„©„Б÷є„Ґ÷≤„®÷ґ„Щ„Ф÷ЄвАО (sho њareha, вАЬher gatekeepersвАЭ) from вАП„©„Б÷є„Ґ÷µ„®вАО (sho њer, вАЬporterвАЭ; BDB 1045 s.v. вАП„©„Б÷є„Ґ÷µ„®вАО). The revocalization creates tight parallelism: вАЬher gatekeepersвАЭ//вАЬher priests,вАЭ but ruins the chiasm: (A) her gatekeepers, (B) her priests, (BвАЩ) her virgins, (AвАЩ) the city itself.
n2500125: tn The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷µ„ЭвАО (shamem) normally means вАЬto be desolated; to be appalled,вАЭ but when used in reference to land, it means вАЬdesertedвАЭ (Isa 49:8; Ezek 33:28; 35:12, 15; 36:4) (BDB 1030 s.v. 1).
n2500126: tn Heb вАЬgroanвАЭ or вАЬsigh.вАЭ The verb вАП„Р÷Є„†÷Ј„ЧвАО ( Њanakh) is an expression of grief (Prov 29:2; Isa 24:7; Lam 1:4, 8; Ezek 9:4; 21:11). BDB 58 s.v. 1 suggests that it means вАЬsigh,вАЭ but HALOT 70-71 s.v. prefers вАЬgroanвАЭ here.
n2500127:
tc The MT reads вАП„†÷Љ„Х÷Љ„Т„Х÷є„™вАО (nugot, вАЬare grievedвАЭ), Niphal participle feminine plural from вАП„Щ÷Є„Т÷Є„ФвАО (yagah, вАЬto grieveвАЭ). The LXX бЉАќ≥ѕМќЉќµќљќ±ќє (agomenai) reflects вАП„†÷Є„Ф„Х÷Љ„Т„Х÷є„™вАО (nahugot, вАЬare led awayвАЭ), Qal passive participle feminine plural from вАП„†÷Є„Ф÷Ј„ТвАО (nahag, вАЬto lead away into exileвАЭ), also reflected in Aquila and Symmachus. The MT reading is an unusual form (see translatorвАЩs note below) and best explains the origin of the LXX, which is a more common root. It would be difficult to explain the origin of the MT reading if the LXX reflected the original. Therefore, the MT is probably the original reading.
tn Heb вАЬare grievedвАЭ or вАЬare worried.вАЭ The unusual form вАП„†÷Љ„Х÷Љ„Т„Х÷є„™вАО (nugot) is probably best explained as Niphal feminine plural participle (with dissimilated nun [вАП„ЯвАО]) from вАП„Щ÷Є„Т÷Є„ФвАО (yagah, вАЬto grieveвАЭ). The similarly formed Niphal participle masculine plural construct вАП„†„Х÷Љ„Т÷µ„ЩвАО (nuge) appears in Zeph 3:18 (GKC 421 ¬І130.a). The Niphal of вАП„Щ÷Є„Т÷Є„ФвАО (yagah, вАЬto grieveвАЭ) appears only twice, both in contexts of sorrow: вАЬto grieve, sorrowвАЭ (Lam 1:4; Zeph 3:18).
n2500128: tn Heb вАЬand she is bitter to herself,вАЭ that is, вАЬsick insideвАЭ (2 Kgs 4:27)
n2500129: tn Heb вАЬher foes became [her] headвАЭ (вАП„Ф÷Є„Щ„Х÷Љ „¶÷Є„®÷ґ„Щ„Ф÷Є „Ь÷∞„®÷є„Р„©„БвАО, hayu tsareha lero Њsh) or more idiomatically вАЬhave come out on top.вАЭ This is a Semitic idiom for domination or subjugation, with вАЬheadвАЭ as a metaphor for leader.
n2500130: tn The nuance expressed in the LXX is that her enemies prosper (cf. KJV, NASB, NRSV, NLT).
n2500131: tn Heb вАЬbecause of her many rebellions.вАЭ The plural вАП„§÷Љ÷∞„©„Б÷Є„Ґ÷ґ„Щ„Ф÷ЄвАО (pesha њeha, вАЬher rebellionsвАЭ) is an example of the plural of repeated action or characteristic behavior (see IBHS 121 ¬І7.4.2c). The third person feminine singular suffix (вАЬherвАЭ) probably functions as a subjective genitive: вАЬher rebellionsвАЭ = вАЬshe has rebelled.вАЭ
n2500132: tn The singular noun вАП„©„Б÷∞„С÷і„ЩвАО (shevi) is a collective singular, meaning вАЬcaptives, prisoners.вАЭ It functions as an adverbial accusative of state: вАЬ[they] went away as captives.вАЭ
n2500133: tn Heb вАЬthe daughter of Zion.вАЭ This phrase is used as an epithet for the city. вАЬDaughterвАЭ may seem extraneous in English but consciously joins the various epithets and metaphors of Jerusalem as a woman, a device used to evoke sympathy from the reader.
n2500134: tn Heb вАЬall her splendor.вАЭ The third person feminine singular pronominal suffix (вАЬherвАЭ) functions as a subjective genitive: вАЬeverything in which she gloried.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ф÷Є„У÷Є„®вАО (hadar, вАЬsplendorвАЭ) is used of personal and impersonal referents in whom Israel gloried: Ephraim (Deut 33:17), Jerusalem (Isa 5:14), Carmel (Isa 35:2). The context focuses on the exile of ZionвАЩs children (1:5c) and leaders (1:6bc). The departure of the children and leaders of Jerusalem going away into exile suggested to the writer the departure of the glory of Israel.
n2500135: tn Heb вАЬIt has gone out from the daughter of Zion, all her splendor.вАЭ
n2500136: tn Heb вАЬthey fled with no strengthвАЭ (вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷µ„Ь÷∞„Ы„Х÷Љ „С÷∞„Ь÷є„Р÷Њ„Ы÷є„Ч÷ЈвАО, vayyelekhu belo Њ khoakh).
n2500137: tn Heb вАЬthe pursuerвАЭ or вАЬthe chaser.вАЭ The term вАП„®÷Є„У÷Ј„£вАО (radaf, вАЬto chase, pursueвАЭ) here refers to a hunter (e.g., 1 Sam 26:20). It is used figuratively (hypocatastasis) of military enemies who вАЬhunt downвАЭ those who flee for their lives (e.g., Gen 14:15; Lev 26:7, 36; Judg 4:22; Pss 7:6; 69:27; 83:16; 143:3; Isa 17:13; Lam 5:5; Amos 1:11).
n2500138: sn As elsewhere in chap. 1, Jerusalem is personified as remembering the catastrophic days of 587 b.c. when Nebuchadnezzar destroyed the city and exiled its inhabitants. Like one of its dispossessed inhabitants, Jerusalem is pictured as becoming impoverished and homeless.
n2500139: tn Heb вАЬthe days of her poverty and her homelessness,вАЭ or вАЬthe days of her affliction and wandering.вАЭ The plural construct вАП„Щ÷∞„Ю÷µ„ЩвАО (yeme, вАЬdays ofвАЭ) functions in the general sense вАЬthe time ofвАЭ or вАЬwhen,вАЭ envisioning the time period in which this occurred. The principal question is whether the phrase is a direct object or an adverb. If a direct object, she remembers either the season when the process happened or she remembers, i.e., reflects on, her current season of life. An adverbial sense, вАЬduringвАЭ or вАЬthroughoutвАЭ normally occurs with вАП„Ы÷Љ÷є„ЬвАО (kol, вАЬallвАЭ) in the phrase вАЬall the days ofвА¶вАЭ but may also occur without вАП„Ы÷Љ÷є„ЬвАО (kol) in poetry as in Job 10:20. The adverbial sense would be translated вАЬduring her poor homeless days.вАЭ Treating вАЬdaysвАЭ adverbially makes better sense with line 7b, whereas treating вАЬdaysвАЭ as a direct object makes better sense with line 7c.
n2500140: tn The third person feminine singular suffixes on the terms вАП„Ґ÷Є„†÷∞„Щ÷Є„Ф÷Љ „Х÷Љ„Ю÷∞„®„Х÷Љ„У÷ґ„Щ„Ф÷ЄвАО ( њonyah umerudeha, вАЬher poverty and her homelessness,вАЭ or вАЬthe days of her affliction and wanderingвАЭ) function as subjective genitives: вАЬshe became impoverished and homeless.вАЭ The plural noun вАП„Х÷Љ„Ю÷∞„®„Х÷Љ„У÷ґ„Щ„Ф÷ЄвАО (umerudeha, lit. вАЬher homelessnessesвАЭ) is an example of the plural of intensity. The two nouns вАП„Ґ÷Є„†÷∞„Щ÷Є„Ф÷Љ „Х÷Љ„Ю÷∞„®„Х÷Љ„У÷ґ„Щ„Ф÷ЄвАО ( њonyah umerudeha, lit., вАЬher poverty and her homelessnessвАЭ) form a nominal hendiadys in which one noun functions adjectivally and the other retains its full nominal sense: вАЬher impoverished homelessnessвАЭ or вАЬhomeless poorвАЭ (GKC 397-98 ¬І124.e). The nearly identical phrase вАП„Ґ÷≤„†÷і„Щ÷Љ÷і„Щ„Э „Ю÷∞„®„Х÷Љ„У÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( њaniyyim merudim, вАЬhomeless poorвАЭ) is used in Isa 58:7 (see GKC 226 ¬І83.c), suggesting this was a Hebrew idiom. Jerusalem is personified as one of its inhabitants who became impoverished and homeless when the city was destroyed.
n2500141: tc The BHS editors suggest that the second bicola in 1:7 is a late addition and should be deleted. Apart from the four sets of bicola here in 1:7 and again in 2:19, every stanza in chapters 1-4 consists of three sets of bicola. Commentators usually suggest dropping line b or line c. Depending on the meaning of вАЬdaysвАЭ in line a (see note on вАЬwhenвАЭ earlier in the verse) either line makes sense. The four lines would make sense as two bicola if вАЬdays ofвАЭ in line 7a is understood adverbially and 7b as the direct object completing the sentence. Lines 7c-d would begin with a temporal modifier and the rest of the couplet describe conditions that were true at that time.
n2500142: tn Heb вАЬinto the hand of.вАЭ In such phrases вАЬhandвАЭ represents power or authority.
n2500143: tn Heb вАЬand there was no helper for her.вАЭ This phrase is used idiomatically in OT to describe the plight of a city whose allies refuse to help ward off a powerful attacker. The nominal participle II вАП„Ґ„Х÷є„Ц÷µ„®вАО ( њozer) refers elsewhere to military warriors (1 Chr 12:1, 18, 22; 2 Chr 20:23; 26:7; 28:23; 26:15; Pss 28:7; 46:6; Ezek 12:14; 30:8; 32:21; Dan 11:34) and the related noun refers to military allies upon whom an attacked city calls for help (Lachish Letters 19:1).
n2500144: tn Heb вАЬthe adversariesвАЭ (вАП„¶÷Є„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО, tsarim). The third person feminine singular pronoun вАЬherвАЭ is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity and good English style.
n2500145: tn The verb вАП„®÷Є„Р÷Є„ФвАО (ra Њah, вАЬto lookвАЭ) has a broad range of meanings, including вАЬto feast the eyes uponвАЭ and вАЬto look down onвАЭ or вАЬto gloat overвАЭ fallen enemies with exultation and triumph (e.g., Judg 16:27; Pss 22:18; 112:8; 118:7; Ezek 28:17; Mic 7:10; Obad 12, 13). This nuance is clarified by the synonymous parallelism between вАП„®÷Є„Р„Х÷Љ„Ф÷ЄвАО (ra Њuha, вАЬthey gloated over herвАЭ) in the A-line and вАП„©„В÷Є„Ч÷≤„І„Х÷Љ „Ґ÷Ј„Ь÷Њ„Ю÷і„©„Б÷∞„С÷Љ÷Ј„™÷Љ÷ґ„Ф÷ЄвАО (sakhaqu  њal mishbatteha, вАЬthey mocked at her downfallвАЭ) in the B-line.
n2500146: tn Heb вАЬlaughedвАЭ or вАЬsneered.вАЭ The verb вАП„©„В÷Є„Ч÷Ј„ІвАО (sakhaq, вАЬto laughвАЭ) is often used in reference to contempt and derision (e.g., Job 30:1; Pss 37:13; 52:8; 59:9; Lam 1:7).
n2500147:
tc The MT reads вАП„Ю÷і„©„Б÷∞„С÷Љ÷Ј„™÷Љ÷ґ„Ф÷ЄвАО (mishbatteha, вАЬher annihilationвАЭ) from the noun вАП„Ю÷і„©„Б÷∞„С÷Љ÷Є„™вАО (mishbat, вАЬcessation, annihilationвАЭ), which is derived from the root вАП„©„Б÷Є„С÷Ј„™вАО (shavat, вАЬto ceaseвАЭ). The LXX mistakenly connected this with the root вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„СвАО (yashav, вАЬto dwellвАЭ), reading ќЉќµѕДќњќєќЇќµѕГќѓбЊ≥ ќ±бљРѕДбњЖѕВ (metoikesia autƒУs) which reflects вАП„©„Б÷і„С÷∞„™÷Љ÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО (shivtah, вАЬher dwellingвАЭ). The MT is favored on the basis of internal evidence: (1) The MT is the more difficult reading, being a hapax legomenon, (2) the LXX is guilty of simply misunderstanding the root and wrongly vocalizing the consonantal text, and (3) the LXX does not make good sense contextually, while the MT does.
tn Heb вАЬher cessationвАЭ or вАЬher annihilation.вАЭ
n2500148: tc The MT reads вАП„Ч÷µ„Ш÷∞„РвАО (khet Њ, вАЬsinвАЭ), but the BHS editors suggest the vocalization вАП„Ч÷Є„Ш÷є„РвАО (khato Њ, вАЬsinвАЭ), Qal infinitive absolute.
n2500149: tn Heb вАЬshe has become an object of head-noddingвАЭ (вАП„Ь÷∞„†÷і„Щ„У÷Є„Ф „Ф÷Є„Щ÷Є„™÷Є„ФвАО, leniydah hayatah). This reflects the ancient Near Eastern custom of shaking the head in scorn (e.g., Jer 18:16; Ps 44:15 [14 HT]), hence the translation вАЬobject of scorn.вАЭ There is debate whether вАП„†÷і„Щ„У÷Є„ФвАО (nidah) means (1) вАЬobject of head-shakingвАЭ from вАП„†„Х÷Љ„УвАО (nud, вАЬto shake,вАЭ BDB 626-27 s.v. вАП„†„Х÷Љ„УвАО); (2) вАЬunclean thingвАЭ from вАП„†÷Є„У÷Ј„ФвАО (nadah, вАЬto be impureвАЭ); or (3) вАЬwandererвАЭ from вАП„†÷Є„У÷Ј„УвАО (nadad, вАЬto wander,вАЭ BDB 622 s.v. I вАП„†÷Є„У÷Ј„УвАО). The LXX and Rashi connected it to вАП„†÷Є„У÷Ј„УвАО (nadad, вАЬto wanderвАЭ); however, several important early Greek recensions (Aquila and Symmachus) and Syriac translated it as вАЬunclean thing.вАЭ The modern English versions are split: (1) вАЬunclean thingвАЭ (NASB); вАЬuncleanвАЭ (NIV); (2) вАЬa mockeryвАЭ (NRSV).
n2500150: sn The Piel participle of вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„С÷µ„УвАО (kaved) is infrequent and usually translated formulaically as those who honor someone. The feminine nuance may be best represented as вАЬher admirers have despised her.вАЭ
n2500151: tn The verb вАП„Ф÷і„Ц÷Љ÷і„Щ„Ь„Х÷Љ„Ф÷ЄвАО (hizziluha) is generally understood as a rare form of Hiphil perfect third person common plural + third person feminine singular suffix from I вАП„Ц÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (zalal, вАЬto despiseвАЭ): вАЬthey despise her.вАЭ This follows the I nun (вАП„ЯвАО) pattern with daghesh (dot) in zayin (вАП„ЦвАО) rather than the expected geminate pattern вАП„Ф÷і„Ц÷і„Щ„Ь÷Љ„Х÷Љ„Ф÷ЄвАО (hizilluha) with daghesh in lamed (вАП„ЬвАО) (GKC 178-79 ¬І67.l).
n2500152: sn The expression have seen her nakedness is a common metaphor to describe the plunder and looting of a city by a conquering army, probably drawn on the ignominious and heinous custom of raping the women of a conquered city as well.
n2500153: tn Heb вАЬgroanвАЭ or вАЬsigh.вАЭ The verb вАП„Р÷Є„†÷Ј„ЧвАО ( Њanakh, appearing only in Niphal) means вАЬsighвАЭ (BDB 58 s.v. 1) or вАЬgroanвАЭ (HALOT 70-71 s.v.) as an expression of grief (Prov 29:2; Isa 24:7; Lam 1:4, 8; Ezek 9:4; 21:11). The word вАП„Т÷Љ÷Ј„ЭвАО (gam) is usually a particle meaning вАЬalso,вАЭ but has been shown from Ugaritic to have the meaning вАЬaloud.вАЭ See T. McDaniel, вАЬPhilological Studies in Lamentations, I-II,вАЭ Bib 49 (1968): 31-32.
n2500155: tn Heb вАЬuncleanness.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ш÷ї„Ю÷∞„Р÷Є„ФвАО (tum Њah, вАЬuncleannessвАЭ) refers in general to the state of ritual uncleanness and specifically to (1) sexual uncleanness (Num 5:19); (2) filthy material (Ezek 24:11; 2 Chr 29:16); (3) ritual uncleanness (Lev 16:16, 19; Ezek 22:15; 24:13; 36:25, 29; 39:24; Zech 13:2); (4) menstrual uncleanness (Lev 15:25, 26, 30; 18:19; Ezek 36:17); and (5) polluted meat (Judg 13:7, 14). Here, Jerusalem is personified as a woman whose menstrual uncleanness has soiled even her own clothes; this is a picture of the consequences of the sin of Jerusalem: uncleanness = her sin, and soiling her own clothes = consequences of sin. The poet may also be mixing metaphors, allowing various images (of shame) to circulate in the hearerвАЩs mind, including rape and public exposure. By not again mentioning sin directly (a topic relatively infrequent in this book), the poet lays a general acknowledgment of sin in 1:8 alongside an exceptionally vivid picture of the horrific circumstances that have come to be. This is no simplistic explanation that sin merits such inhumane treatment. Instead 1:9 insists that no matter the legal implications of being guilty, the Lord should be motivated to aid Jerusalem (and therefore her people) because her obscene reality is so revolting.
n2500156: tn Heb вАЬher uncleanness is in her skirts.вАЭ
n2500157: tn Heb вАЬher skirts.вАЭ This term is a synecdoche of specific (skirts) for general (clothing).
n2500158: tn The basic meaning of вАП„Ц÷Є„Ы÷Ј„®вАО (zakhar) is вАЬto remember, call to mindвАЭ (HALOT 270 s.v. I вАП„Ц„Ы„®вАО). Although it is often used in reference to recollection of past events or consideration of present situations, it also may mean вАЬto consider, think aboutвАЭ the future outcome of conduct (e.g., Isa 47:7) (BDB 270 s.v. 5). The same term is used in Lam 1:7a.
n2500159: tn Heb вАЬshe did not consider her end.вАЭ The noun вАП„Р÷Ј„Ч÷≤„®÷і„Щ„™вАО ( Њakharit, вАЬendвАЭ) here refers to an outcome or the consequences of an action; in light of 1:8, here it is the consequence of sin or immoral behavior (Num 23:10; 24:20; Deut 32:20, 29; Job 8:7; Pss 37:37; 73:17; Prov 14:12; 23:32; 25:8; Eccl 7:8; Isa 46:10; 47:7; Jer 5:31; 17:11; Dan 12:8).
n2500160:
tc The MT reads вАП„Х÷Ј„™÷Љ÷µ„®÷ґ„УвАО (vattered), vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive + Qal preterite third person feminine singular from вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷Ј„УвАО (yarad, вАЬto go downвАЭ). Symmachus has ќЇќ±бљґ ќЇќ±ѕДбљµѕЗќЄќЈ (kai katƒУchthƒУ, вАЬand she was brought downвАЭ) and Vulgate deposita est, passive forms that might reflect вАП„Х÷Ј„™÷Љ„Х÷Љ„®÷Ј„УвАО (vatturad, vav consecutive + Pual preterite third person feminine singular from from вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷Ј„УвАО [yarad, вАЬto go downвАЭ]). External evidence favors the MT (supported by all other ancient versions and medieval Hebrew mss); none of the other ancient versions preserve/reflect a passive form. Symmachus is known to have departed from a wooden literal translation (characteristic of Aquila) in favor of smooth and elegant Greek style. The second edition of the Latin Vulgate drew on Symmachus; thus, it is not an independent witness to the passive reading, but merely a secondary witness reflecting Symmachus. The MT is undoubtedly the original reading.
tn Heb вАЬand she came down in an astonishing way,вАЭ or вАЬand she was brought down in an astonishing way.вАЭ
n2500161: tn The noun вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„Ь÷ґ„РвАО (pele Њ) means not only вАЬmiracle, wonderвАЭ (BDB 810 s.v.) but вАЬsomething unusual, astonishingвАЭ (HALOT 928 s.v.). The plural вАП„§÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷Є„Р÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (pela Њim, lit., вАЬastonishmentsвАЭ) is an example of the plural of intensity: вАЬvery astonishing.вАЭ The noun functions as an adverbial accusative of manner; the nature of her descent shocks and astounds. Rendering вАП„§÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷Є„Р÷і„Щ„ЭвАО вАП„Х÷Ј„™÷Љ÷µ„®÷ґ„УвАО (vattered pela Њim) as вАЬshe has come down marvelouslyвАЭ (cf. BDB 810 s.v. 1 and KJV, ASV) is hardly appropriate; it is better to nuance it as вАЬin an astonishing wayвАЭ (HALOT 928 s.v. 3) or simply as вАЬwas astonishing.вАЭ
n2500162: tn The words вАЬshe criedвАЭ do not appear in the Hebrew. They are added to indicate that personified Jerusalem is speaking.
n2500163: tc The MT reads вАП„Ґ÷Є„†÷∞„Щ÷і„ЩвАО ( њonyi, вАЬmy afflictionвАЭ) as reflected in all the ancient versions (LXX, Aramaic Targum, Latin Vulgate, Syriac Peshitta) and the medieval Hebrew mss. The Bohairic version and Ambrosius, however, read вАЬher affliction,вАЭ which led the BHS editors to suggest a Vorlage of вАП„Ґ÷Є„†÷∞„Щ÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО ( њonyah, вАЬher afflictionвАЭ). External evidence strongly favors the MT reading. The third person feminine singular textual variant probably arose out of an attempt to harmonize this form with all the other third person feminine singular forms in 1:1-11a. The MT is undoubtedly the original reading.
n2500164: tn Heb вАЬan enemy.вАЭ While it is understood that the enemy is JerusalemвАЩs, not using the pronoun in Hebrew leaves room to imply to God that the enemy is not only JerusalemвАЩs but also GodвАЩs.
n2500165: tn Heb вАЬstretched out his hand.вАЭ The war imagery is of seizure of property; the anthropomorphic element pictures rape. This is an idiom that describes greedy actions (BDB 831 s.v. вАП„§÷Є„®÷Ј„©„ВвАО), meaning вАЬto seizeвАЭ (HALOT 976 s.v. 2).
n2500166:
tc The Kethib is written вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ч÷≤„Ю„Х÷є„У÷Љ÷µ„Щ„Ф÷ґ„ЭвАО (makhamodehem, вАЬher desired thingsвАЭ); the Qere and many medieval Hebrew mss read вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ч÷≤„Ю÷Ј„У÷Љ÷µ„Щ„Ф÷ґ„ЭвАО (makhamaddehem, вАЬher desirable thingsвАЭ). The Qere reading should be adopted.
tn Heb вАЬall her desirable things.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ч÷∞„Ю÷Є„УвАО (makhmad, вАЬdesirable thingвАЭ) refers to valuable possessions, such as gold and silver, that people desire (e.g., Ezra 8:27). This probably refers, not to the valuable possessions of Jerusalem in general, but to the sacred objects in the temple in particular, as suggested by the rest of the verse. For the anthropomorphic image compare Song 5:16.
n2500167: tn Heb вАЬshe watched,вАЭ or вАЬshe saw.вАЭ The verb вАП„®÷Є„Р÷Є„ФвАО (ra Њah, вАЬto seeвАЭ) has a broad range of meanings, including вАЬto seeвАЭ a spectacle causing grief (Gen 21:16; 44:34; Num 11:15; 2 Kgs 22:20; 2 Chr 34:28; Esth 8:6) or abhorrence (Isa 66:24). The words вАЬin horrorвАЭ are added to вАЬshe watchedвАЭ to bring out this nuance.
n2500168: sn The syntax of the sentence is interrupted by the insertion of the following sentence, вАЬthey invadedвА¶,вАЭ then continued with вАЬwhomвА¶вАЭ The disruption of the syntax is a structural device intended to help convey the shock of the situation.
n2500169: tn Heb вАЬher sanctuary.вАЭ The term вАП„Ю÷і„І÷∞„У÷Љ÷Є„©„Б÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО (miqdashah, вАЬher sanctuaryвАЭ) refers to the temple. Anthropomorphically, translating as вАЬher sacred placeвАЭ would also allow for the rape imagery.
n2500170: sn Lam 1-2 has two speaking voices: a third person voice reporting the horrific reality of JerusalemвАЩs suffering and JerusalemвАЩs voice. See W. F. Lanahan, вАЬThe Speaking Voice in the Book of LamentationsвАЭ JBL 93 (1974): 41-49. The reporting voice has been addressing the listener, referring to the Lord in the third person. Here he switches to a second person address to God, also changing the wording of the following command to second person. The revulsion of the reporter is so great that he is moved to address God directly.
n2500171: tn Heb вАЬenter.вАЭ The Hebrew term вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„РвАО (bo Њ) is also a sexual metaphor.
n2500172:
tn The noun вАП„І÷Є„Ф÷Є„ЬвАО (qahal, вАЬassemblyвАЭ) does not refer here to the collective group of people assembled to worship the Lord, but to the place of their assembly: the temple. This is an example of a synecdoche of the people contained (= assembly) for the container (= temple). The intent is to make the violation feel more personal than someone walking into a building.
sn This is a quotation from Deut 23:3: вАЬNo Ammonite or Moabite or any of his descendants may enter the assembly of the Lord, even down to the tenth generation.вАЭ Jeremiah applies this prohibition against Ammonites and Moabites to the Babylonians, who ransacked and destroyed the temple in 587/586 b.c. This hermeneutical move may be explained on the basis of synecdoche of species (= Ammonites and Moabites) for general (= unconverted Gentiles as a whole). On a different note, the prohibition forbidding Ammonites and Moabites from entering the вАЬassemblyвАЭ (вАП„І÷Є„Ф÷Є„ЬвАО, qahal, Deut 23:2-8) did not disallow Gentile proselytes from converting to Yahwism or from living within the community (= assembled body) of Israel. For example, Ruth the Moabitess abandoned the worship of Moabite gods and embraced Yahweh, then was welcomed into the community of Bethlehem in Judah (Ruth 1:15-22) and even incorporated into the lineage leading to King David (Ruth 4:18-22). This Deuteronomic law did not disallow such genuine conversions of repentant faith toward Yahweh, nor their incorporation into the life of the Israelite community. Nor did it discourage Gentiles from offering sacrifices to the Lord (Num 15:15-16). Rather, it prohibited Gentiles from entering into the tabernacle/temple (= place of assembly) of Israel. This is clear from the reaction of the post-exilic community when it realized that Deut 23:3-5 had been violated by Tobiah the Ammonite, who had been given living quarters in the temple precincts (Neh 13:1-9). This is also reflected in the days of the Second Temple when Gentile proselytes were allowed to enter the вАЬcourt of the GentilesвАЭ in HerodвАЩs temple but were forbidden further access into the inner temple precincts.
n2500173: tn Heb вАЬbread.вАЭ In light of its parallelism with вАП„Р÷є„Ы÷ґ„ЬвАО ( Њokhel, вАЬfoodвАЭ) in the following line, it is possible that вАП„Ь÷ґ„Ч÷ґ„ЭвАО (lekhem, вАЬbreadвАЭ) is used in its broader sense of food or nourishment.
n2500175: tn Heb вАЬtheir desirable things.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ч÷∞„Ю÷Є„УвАО (makhmad, вАЬdesirable thingвАЭ) refers to valuable possessions, such as gold and silver, that people desire (e.g., Ezra 8:27).
n2500176: tn The preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) denotes the purchase price paid for an object (BDB 90 s.v. вАП„С÷Љ÷∞вАО III.3; HALOT 105 s.v. вАП„С÷Љ÷∞вАО 17) (e.g., Gen 23:9; 29:18, 20; 30:16; Lev 25:37; Deut 21:14; 2 Sam 24:24).
n2500177: tn The noun вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh) may originally have referred to the windpipe opening for breathing but came to have associated meanings such as living being, breath, soul, and life (for the latter, see Gen 44:30; Exod 21:23; 2 Sam 14:7; Jon 1:14). When used with вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh), the Hiphil вАП„Ф÷Є„©„Б÷і„Щ„СвАО (hashiv) of вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„СвАО (shuv, вАЬto turn, returnвАЭ) may mean вАЬto restore a personвАЩs vitality,вАЭ that is, to keep a person alive (Lam 1:14, 19).
n2500178: sn The dagesh lene in вАП„Ы÷Љ„ЩвАО (ki) following the vowel ending the verb вАП„Х÷∞„Ф÷Ј„С÷Љ÷і„Щ„Ш÷Є„ФвАО (vehabbitah, вАЬconsiderвАЭ) indicates a dramatic pause between calling for the LordвАЩs attention and stating the allegation to be seen and considered.
n2500179: tc The Heb вАП„Ь„Х÷є„Р „Р÷≤„Ь÷µ„Щ„Ы÷ґ„ЭвАО (lo Њ  Њalekhem, вАЬnot to youвАЭ) is often considered awkward and difficult, but there is no textual evidence yet adduced to suggest a better original reading.
n2500180: tn The line as it stands is imbalanced, such that the reference to the passersby may belong here or as a vocative with the following verb translated вАЬlook.вАЭ
n2500181: tn Heb вАЬHe.вАЭ The personal pronoun вАЬheвАЭ and the personal name вАЬthe Lord,вАЭ both appearing in this verse, are transposed in the translation for the sake of readability. In the Hebrew text, вАЬHeвАЭ appears in the A-line and вАЬthe LordвАЭ appears in the B-lineвАФgood Hebrew poetic style, but awkward English style.
n2500182: tn Heb вАЬwhich was afflicted on me.вАЭ The Polal of вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО ( њalal) gives the passive voice of the Polel. The Polel of the verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО occurs ten times in the Bible, appearing in agricultural passages for gleaning or some other harvest activity and also in military passages. Jer 6:9 plays on this by comparing an attack to gleaning. The relationship between the meaning in the two types of contexts is unclear, but the very neutral rendering вАЬto treatвАЭ in some dictionaries and translations misses the nuance appropriate to the military setting. Indeed, it is not at all feasible in a passage like Judg 20:45, where вАЬthey treated them on the highwayвАЭ would make no sense but вАЬthey mowed them down on the highwayвАЭ would fit the context. Accordingly, the verb is sometimes rendered вАЬtreat violentlyвАЭ or вАЬdeal severely,вАЭ as HALOT 834 s.v. poel.3 suggests for Lam 3:51, although simply suggesting вАЬto deal withвАЭ in Lam 1:22 and 2:20. A more injurious nuance is given to the translation here and in 1:22; 2:20; 3:51.
n2500183: sn The delay in naming the Lord as cause is dramatic. The natural assumption upon hearing the passive verb in the previous line, вАЬit was dealt severely,вАЭ might well be the pillaging army, but instead the Lord is named as the tormentor.
n2500184: tn Heb вАЬin the day of.вАЭ The construction вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Щ„Х÷є„ЭвАО (beyom, вАЬin the day ofвАЭ) is a common Hebrew idiom, meaning вАЬwhenвАЭ or вАЬon the occasion ofвАЭ (e.g., Gen 2:4; Lev 7:35; Num 3:1; Deut 4:15; 2 Sam 22:1; Pss 18:1; 138:3; Zech 8:9).
n2500185: tn Heb вАЬon the day of burning anger.вАЭ
n2500186: tn Heb вАЬHe sent fire from on high.вАЭ Normally God sends fire from heaven. The idiom вАП„Ю÷і„Ю÷Љ÷Є„®„Х÷є„ЭвАО (mimmarom, вАЬfrom on highвАЭ) can still suggest the location but as an idiom may focus on the quality of the referent. For example, вАЬto speak from on highвАЭ means вАЬto presume to speak as if from heavenвАЭ = arrogantly (Ps 73:8); вАЬthey fight against me from on highвАЭ = proudly (Ps 56:3) (BDB 928-29 s.v. вАП„Ю÷Є„®„Х÷є„ЭвАО). As a potential locative, вАП„Ю÷і„Ю÷Љ÷Є„®„Х÷є„ЭвАО designates God as the agent; idiomatically the same term paints him as pitiless.
n2500187: tc The MT reads вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷і„®÷∞„У÷Љ÷ґ„†÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (vayyirdennah, вАЬit prevailed against themвАЭ), representing a vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive + Qal preterite third person masculine singular + third person feminine plural suffix from вАП„®÷Є„У÷Є„ФвАО (radah, вАЬto prevailвАЭ). The LXX form ќЇќ±ѕДбљµќ≥ќ±ќ≥ќµќљ ќ±бљРѕДѕМ (katƒУgagen auto, вАЬit descendedвАЭ) reflects an alternate vocalization tradition of вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷є„®÷і„У÷ґ„†÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (vayyoridennah, вАЬit descended against themвАЭ), representing a vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive + Hiphil preterite third person masculine singular + third person feminine plural suffix from вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷Є„УвАО (yarad, вАЬto go downвАЭ), or вАП„Ф÷є„®÷і„Щ„У÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО (horidah, вАЬit descended against herвАЭ), a Hiphil perfect ms + third person feminine singular suffix from from вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷Є„УвАО (yarad, вАЬto go downвАЭ). Internal evidence favors the MT. The origin of the LXX vocalization can be explained by the influence of the preceding line: вАЬHe sent down fire from on high.вАЭ
n2500188: tn Heb вАЬnet.вАЭ The term вАЬtrapperвАЩsвАЭ is supplied in the translation as a clarification.
n2500189:
tc The consonantal text вАП„†„©„І„У „Ґ„Ь „§„©„Ґ„ЩвАО (nsqd  њl ps њy) is vocalized by the MT as вАП„†÷і„©„В÷∞„І÷Ј„У „Ґ÷є„Ь „§÷Љ÷∞„©„Б÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ЩвАО (nisqad  њol pesha њay, вАЬmy transgression is bound by a yokeвАЭ); but the ancient versions (LXX, Aramaic Targum, Latin Vulgate, Syriac Peshitta) and many medieval Hebrew mss vocalize the text as вАП„†÷і„©„Б÷∞„І÷Ј„У „Ґ÷Ј„Ь „§÷Љ÷∞„©„Б÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ЩвАО (nishqad  њal pesha њay, вАЬwatch is kept upon my transgressionвАЭ). There are two textual deviations: (1) the MT vocalizes the verb as вАП„†÷і„©„В÷∞„І÷Ј„УвАО (nisqad, Niphal perfect third person masculine singular from вАП„©„В÷Є„І÷Ј„УвАО [saqad, вАЬto bindвАЭ]), while the alternate tradition vocalizes it as вАП„†÷і„©„Б÷∞„І÷Ј„УвАО (nishqad, Niphal perfect third person masculine singular from вАП„©„Б÷Є„І÷Ј„УвАО [shaqad, вАЬto keep watchвАЭ]); and (2) the MT vocalizes вАП„Ґ„ЬвАО ( њl) as the noun вАП„Ґ÷є„ЬвАО ( њol, вАЬyokeвАЭ), while the ancient versions and medieval Hebrew mss vocalize it as the preposition вАП„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО ( њal, вАЬuponвАЭ). External evidence favors the alternate vocalization: all the early versions (LXX, Targum, Vulgate, Peshitta) and many medieval Hebrew mss versus the relatively late MT vocalization tradition. However, internal evidence favors the MT vocalization: (1) The MT verb вАП„©„В÷Є„І÷Ј„УвАО (saqad, вАЬto bindвАЭ) is a hapax legomenon (BDB 974 s.v. вАП„©„В÷Є„І÷Ј„УвАО) which might have been easily confused for the more common verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„І÷Є„УвАО (shaqad, вАЬto keep watchвАЭ), which is well attested elsewhere (Job 21:32; Pss 102:8; 127:1; Prov 8:34; Isa 29:20; Jer 1:12; 5:6; 31:28; 44:27; Ezr 8:29; Dan 9:14) (BDB 1052 s.v. вАП„©„В÷Є„І÷Ј„УвАО Qal.2). (2) The syntax of the MT is somewhat awkward, which might have influenced a scribe toward the alternate vocalization. (3) The presence of the noun вАП„Ґ÷ї„Ь÷Љ„Х÷євАО ( њullo, вАЬhis yokeвАЭ) in the following line supports the presence of the same term in this line. (4) Thematic continuity of 1:14 favors the MT: throughout the verse, the inhabitants of Jerusalem are continually compared to yoked animals who are sold into the hands of cruel task-masters. The alternate vocalization intrudes into an otherwise unified stanza. In summary, despite strong external evidence in favor of the alternate vocalization tradition, even stronger internal evidence favors the MT.
tn Heb вАЬmy transgressions are bound with a yoke.вАЭ
n2500190: tc The MT reads вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ь„Х÷ЉвАО ( њalu, вАЬthey went upвАЭ), Qal perfect third person common plural from вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО ( њalah, вАЬto go upвАЭ). However, several important recensions of the LXX reflect an alternate vocalization tradition: Lucian and Symmachus both reflect a Vorlage of вАП„Ґ÷ї„Ь÷Љ„Х÷євАО ( њullo, вАЬhis yokeвАЭ), the noun вАП„Ґ÷є„ЬвАО ( њol, вАЬyokeвАЭ) + third person masculine singular suffix. The Lucianic recension was aimed at bringing the LXX into closer conformity to the Hebrew; therefore, this is an important textual witness. Internal evidence favors the readings of Lucian and Symmachus as well: the entire stanza focuses on the repeated theme of the вАЬyokeвАЭ of the Lord. The MT reading is obscure in meaning, and the third person common plural form violates the syntactical flow: вАЬ[my sins] are lashed together by his hand; they have gone up upon my neck. He has weakened my strength; the Lord has handed me overвА¶.вАЭ On the other hand, the Lucian/Symmachus reading reflects contextual congruence: вАЬMy sins are bound around my neck like a yoke; they are lashed together by his hand. His yoke is upon my neck; he has weakened my strength. He has handed me over to those whom I am powerless to resist.вАЭ
n2500191: tn Heb вАЬhis yoke is upon my neck.вАЭ
n2500192: tn Heb вАЬhe has caused my strength to stumble.вАЭ The phrase вАП„Ф÷і„Ы÷∞„©„Б÷і„Щ„Ь „Ы÷Љ÷є„Ч÷і„ЩвАО (hikhshil kokhi, вАЬHe has made my strength stumbleвАЭ) is an idiom that means вАЬto weaken, make feeble.вАЭ
n2500193: tc Here the MT reads вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay, вАЬthe LordвАЭ), the perpetual Qere reading for вАП„Щ„Ф„Х„ФвАО (YHWH, вАЬYahwehвАЭ), but a multitude of Hebrew mss read consonantal вАП„Щ„Ф„Х„ФвАО (YHWH, traditionally translated вАЬthe LordвАЭ).
n2500194: tn Heb вАЬThe Lord has given me into the hands of.вАЭ
n2500195: tn The verb вАП„°÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ФвАО (salah) occurs only twice in OT, once in Qal (Ps 119:118) and once here in Piel. It is possibly a by-form of вАП„°÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (salal, вАЬto heap upвАЭ). It may also be related to Aramaic вАП„°„Ь„РвАО (sl Њ), meaning вАЬto throw away,вАЭ and Assyrian salu/shalu, meaning вАЬto hurl (away)вАЭ (AHw 1152) or вАЬto kick up dust, shoot (arrows), reject, throw away?вАЭ (CAD 17:272). With people as its object shalu is used of people casting away their children, specifically meaning selling them on the market. The LXX translates вАП„°÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ФвАО (salah) as бЉРќЊбњЖѕБќµќљ (exƒУren, вАЬto remove, lead awayвАЭ). Thus God is either (1) heaping them up (dead) in the city square, (2) putting them up for sale in the city square, or (3) leading them out of the city (into exile or to deprive it of defenders prior to attack). The English вАЬround upвАЭ could accommodate any of these and is also a cattle term, which fits well with the use of the word вАЬbullsвАЭ (see following note).
n2500196: tn Heb вАЬbulls.вАЭ Metaphorically, bulls may refer to mighty ones, leaders, or warriors. F. W. Dobbs-Allsopp (Lamentations [IBC], 69) insightfully suggests that the Samek stanza presents an overarching dissonance by using terms associated with a celebratory feast (bulls, assembly, and a winepress) in sentences where God is abusing the normally expected celebrants, i.e., the вАЬleadersвАЭ are the sacrifice.
n2500197: tc The MT reads вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) here rather than вАП„Щ„Ф„Х„ФвАО (YHWH, вАЬthe LordвАЭ); this occurs again a second time later in this verse. See the tc note at 1:14.
n2500198: tn The verb is elided and understood from the preceding colon. Naming вАЬmy LordвАЭ as the subject of the verb late, as it were, emphasizes the irony of the action taken by a person in this position.
n2500199:
tc The MT reads the preposition вАП„С÷ЉвАО (bet, вАЬinвАЭ) prefixed to вАП„І÷і„®÷∞„С÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (qirbi, вАЬmy midstвАЭ): вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„І÷і„®÷∞„С÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (beqirbi, вАЬin my midstвАЭ); however, the LXX reads бЉРќЇ ќЉќ≠ѕГќњѕЕ ќЉќњѕЕ (ek mesou mou) which may reflect a Vorlage of the preposition вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min, вАЬfromвАЭ): вАП„Ю÷і„І÷Љ÷і„®÷∞„С÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (miqqirbi, вАЬfrom my midstвАЭ). The LXX may have chosen бЉРќЇ to accommodate understanding вАП„°÷і„Ь÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (sillah) as бЉРќЊбњЖѕБќµќљ (exƒУren, вАЬto remove, lead awayвАЭ). The textual deviation may have been caused by an unusual orthographic confusion.
tn Or вАЬout of my midst.вАЭ See the preceding tc note.
n25001100: tn Heb вАЬan assembly.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ю„Х÷є„Ґ÷µ„УвАО (mo њed, вАЬassemblyвАЭ) is normally used in reference to the annual religious festive assemblies of Israel (Ezek 45:17; Hos 9:5; Zeph 3:18; Zech 8:19), though a number of English versions take this вАЬassemblyвАЭ to refer to the invading army that attacks the city (e.g., NAB, NIV, TEV, NLT).
n25001101: tn Heb вАЬa winepress he has stomped.вАЭ The noun вАП„Т÷Љ÷Ј„™вАО (gat, вАЬwinepressвАЭ) functions as an adverbial accusative of location: вАЬin a winepress.вАЭ The translation reflects the synecdoche that is involvedвАФone stomps the grapes that are in the winepress, not the winepress itself.
n25001102: sn The expression the virgin daughter, Judah is used as an epithet, i.e., Virgin Judah or Maiden Judah, further reinforcing the feminine anthrpomorphism.
n25001103:
tc The MT and several medieval Hebrew mss read вАП„Ґ÷µ„Щ„†÷і„Щ „Ґ÷µ„Щ„†÷і„ЩвАО ( њeni,  њeni, вАЬmy eye, my eyeвАЭ). However, the second вАП„Ґ÷µ„Щ„†÷і„ЩвАО does not appear in several other medieval Hebrew mss, or in Old Greek, Syriac Peshitta, or Latin Vulgate.
tn Heb вАЬMy eye, my eye.вАЭ The Hebrew text repeats the term for literary emphasis to stress the emotional distress of personified Jerusalem.
n25001104: tn Heb вАЬwith water.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (mayim, вАЬwaterвАЭ) functions as an adverbial accusative of manner or impersonal instrument. The term вАП„Ю÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (mayim, вАЬwaterвАЭ) is a metonymy of material (= water) for the thing formed (= tears).
n25001105: tn Heb вАЬFor a comforter is far from me.вАЭ
n25001106: tn The phrase вАП„Ю÷µ„©„Б÷і„Щ„С „†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷і„ЩвАО (meshiv nafshi, вАЬone who could cause my soul to returnвАЭ) is a Hebrew idiom that means вАЬone who could encourage me.вАЭ The noun вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷і„ЩвАО (nafshi) refers to the whole person (e.g., Gen 27:4, 25; 49:6; Lev 26:11, 30; Num 23:10; Judg 5:21; 16:30; Isa 1:14; Lam 3:24). When used with the noun вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh), the Hiphil вАП„Ф÷Є„©„Б÷і„Щ„СвАО (hashiv) of вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„СвАО (shuv, вАЬto turn, returnвАЭ) means вАЬto encourage, refresh, cheerвАЭ a person emotionally (Ruth 4:15; Pss 19:8; 23:3; Prov 25:13; Lam 1:11, 16, 19).
n25001107: tn Heb вАЬmy sons.вАЭ The term вАЬmy sonsвАЭ (вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„†÷Ј„ЩвАО, banay) is a figurative description (hypocatastasis) of the former inhabitants of Jerusalem/Judah personified as the Lady JerusalemвАЩs children. Jerusalem mourns (and views) their devastation like a mother would her children.
n25001108: tn The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷µ„ЭвАО (shamem) means вАЬto be desolated.вАЭ The verb is used used in reference to land destroyed in battle and left вАЬdesertedвАЭ (Isa 49:8; Ezek 33:28; 35:12, 15; 36:4). When used in reference to persons, it describes the aftermath of a physical attack, such as rape (2 Sam 13:20) or military overthrow of a city (Isa 54:1; Lam 1:13, 16; 3:11).
n25001109: tn Heb вАЬhis neighbors,вАЭ which refers to the surrounding nations.
n25001110: tn The noun II вАП„†÷і„У÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (niddah, вАЬunclean thingвАЭ) has three basic categories of meaning: (1) biological uncleanness: menstruation of a woman (Lev 12:2, 5; 15:19-33 [9x]; Num 19:9, 13, 20; 31:23; Ezek 18:6; 22:10; 36:17); (2) ceremonial uncleanness: moral impurity and idolatry (Lev 20:21; 2 Chr 29:5; Ezra 9:11; Zech 13:1); and (3) physical uncleanness: filthy garbage (Lam 1:17; Ezek 7:19, 20).
n25001111:
tc The MT reads вАП„С÷Љ÷µ„Щ„†÷µ„Щ„Ф÷ґ„ЭвАО (benehem, вАЬin themвАЭ = вАЬin their midstвАЭ). The BHS editors suggest that this is a textual variation from an original text of вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ґ÷µ„Щ„†÷µ„Щ„Ф÷ґ„ЭвАО (be њenehem, вАЬin their eyesвАЭ = вАЬin their viewвАЭ). The вАП„ҐвАО ( њayin) might have dropped out due to orthographic confusion.
tn Or вАЬin their eyes.вАЭ See the preceding tc note.
n25001112: tn Heb вАЬThe Lord himself is right.вАЭ The phrase вАЬto judge meвАЭ is not in the Hebrew but is added in the translation to clarify the expression.
n25001113: tn Heb вАЬHis mouth.вАЭ The term вАЬmouthвАЭ (вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО, peh) is a metonymy of instrument (= mouth) for the product (= words). The term вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО often stands for spoken words (Ps 49:14; Eccl 10:3; Isa 29:13), declaration (Gen 41:40; Exod 38:21; Num 35:30; Deut 17:6; Ezra 1:1) and commands of God (Exod 17:1; Num 14:41; 22:18; Josh 15:13; 1 Sam 15:24; 1 Chr 12:24; Prov 8:29; Isa 34:16; 62:2). When the verb вАП„Ю÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (marah, вАЬto rebelвАЭ) is used with вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (peh, вАЬmouthвАЭ) as the direct object, it connotes disobedience to GodвАЩs commandments (Num 20:24; 1 Sam 12:14, 15; 1 Kgs 13:21) (BDB 805 s.v. вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО 2.c).
n25001114:
tc The Kethib is written вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( њammim, вАЬpeoplesвАЭ), but the Qere, followed by many medieval Hebrew mss and the ancient versions (LXX and Aramaic Targum), reads вАП„Ф÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (ha њammim, вАЬO peoplesвАЭ). The Qere is probably the original reading.
tn Heb вАЬO peoples.вАЭ Here Jerusalem addresses the peoples of the surrounding nations (note the use of вАЬneighborsвАЭ in the preceding verse).
n25001115: sn The term вАЬloversвАЭ is a figurative expression (hypocatastasis), comparing JerusalemвАЩs false gods and political alliance with Assyria to a womanвАЩs immoral lovers. The prophet Hosea uses similar imagery (Hos 2:5, 7, 10, 13).
n25001116: tn Here the conjunction вАП„Ы÷Љ„ЩвАО (ki) functions either (1) with a temporal sense in reference to a past event, following a perfect: вАЬwhenвАЭ (BDB 473 s.v. 2.a; cf. KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV), (2) with a concessive sense, following a perfect: вАЬalthoughвАЭ (Pss 21:12; 119:83; Mic 7:8; Nah 1:10; cf. BDB 473 s.v. 2.c.ќ≤), or (3) with an intensive force, introducing a statement with emphasis: вАЬsurely, certainlyвАЭ (BDB 472 s.v. 1.e). The present translation follows the third option.
n25001117: tn The vav (вАП„ХвАО) prefixed to вАП„Х÷∞„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷і„Щ„С„Х÷ЉвАО (veyashivu) introduces a purpose clause: вАЬthey sought food for themselves, in order to keep themselves alive.вАЭ
n25001118: tn The noun вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh, вАЬsoulвАЭ) functions as a metonymy of association (= life) (e.g., Gen 44:30; Exod 21:23; 2 Sam 14:7; Jon 1:14). When used with вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh), the Hiphil вАП„Ф÷Є„©„Б÷і„Щ„СвАО (hashiv) of вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„СвАО (shuv, вАЬto turn, returnвАЭ) may mean вАЬto preserve a personвАЩs life,вАЭ that is, to keep a person alive (Lam 1:14, 19).
n25001119: tc The LXX adds ќЇќ±бљґ ќњбљРѕЗ ќµбљЧѕБќњќљ (kai ouch heuron, вАЬbut they did not find itвАЭ). This is probably an explanatory scribal gloss, added to explicate what appeared to be ambiguous. The LXX often adds explanatory glosses in many OT books.
n25001120: tn Heb вАЬbecause distress belongs to meвАЭ (вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„Щ÷Њ„¶÷Ј„®÷Њ„Ь÷і„ЩвАО, ki tsar li).
n25001121: tn Heb вАЬmy bowels burn,вАЭ or вАЬmy bowels are in a ferment.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ч÷≥„Ю÷Ј„®÷∞„Ю÷Є„®„Х÷ЉвАО (khamarmaru) is an unusual form and derived from a debated root: Poalal perfect third person common plural from III вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю÷Ј„®вАО (khamar, вАЬto be red,вАЭ HALOT 330 s.v. III вАП„Ч„Ю„®вАО) or Pe њal њal perfect third person common plural from I вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю÷Ј„®вАО (khamar, вАЬto ferment, boil up,вАЭ BDB 330 s.v. I вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю÷Ј„®вАО). The Poalal stem of this verb occurs only three times in the OT: with вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„†÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (panim, вАЬface,вАЭ Job 16:16) and вАП„Ю÷µ„Ґ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (me њim, вАЬbowels,вАЭ Lam 1:20; 2:11). The phrase вАП„Ю÷µ„Ґ÷Ј„Щ „Ч÷≥„Ю÷Ј„®÷∞„Ю÷Є„®„Х÷ЉвАО (me њay khamarmaru) means вАЬmy bowels burnedвАЭ (HALOT 330 s.v.) or вАЬmy bowels are in a ferment,вАЭ as a euphemism for lower-intestinal bowel problems (BDB 330 s.v.). This phrase also occurs in later rabbinic literature (m. Sanhedrin 7:2). The present translation, вАЬmy stomach is in knots,вАЭ is not a literal equivalent to this Hebrew idiom; however, it is an attempt to approximate the equivalent English idiom.
n25001122: tn The Niphl participle from вАП„Ф÷Є„§÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (hafakh, вАЬto turn overвАЭ) functions verbally, referring to progressive present-time action (from the speakerвАЩs viewpoint).
n25001123: tn Heb вАЬbecause I was certainly rebellious.вАЭ Using the infinitive absolute before the finite verb of the same root emphasizes the verbвАЩs modality, here indicative mode.
n25001124: tn Heb вАЬin the street the sword bereaves.вАЭ The words вАЬa mother of her childrenвАЭ are supplied in the translation as a clarification.
n25001125: tn Heb вАЬin the house it is like death.вАЭ
n25001126: tc The MT reads вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷∞„Ґ„Х÷ЉвАО (shame њu, вАЬThey heardвАЭ), Qal perfect third person common plural from вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ҐвАО (shama њ, вАЬto hearвАЭ). The LXX бЉАќЇќњѕНѕГќ±ѕДќµ (akousate) reflects вАП„©„Б÷і„Ю÷∞„Ґ„Х÷ЉвАО (shim њu, вАЬHear!вАЭ), the imperative second person masculine plural form of the same stem and root. Most English versions follow the MT (KJV, NASB, NIV, NJPS, CEV), but several follow the LXX (RSV, NRSV, TEV). Internal evidence favors the MT. The poet has been addressing God (v. 20) and continues to describe his distress, including what the enemy does. The description later in this verse also uses the Qal perfect third person common plural form вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷∞„Ґ„Х÷ЉвАО (shame њu, вАЬthey heardвАЭ). The MT vocalization is most likely original.
n25001127: tn вАЬYouвАЭ here and in the following line refers to the Lord.
n25001129: tc The MT reads вАП„Ф÷µ„С÷µ„Р„™÷ЄвАО (heve Њta, вАЬyou broughtвАЭ) and is followed by the LXX. The Syriac Peshitta translates the verb with an imperative, implying an original text of вАП„Ф÷Є„С÷µ„Р „Р÷µ„™вАО (have Њ  Њet), the imperative plus direct-object indicator. The MTвАЩs reading would arise from dropping an вАП„РвАО ( Њalef) followed by wrong word division. An alternate view is to understand the perfect as precative, a proposed unusual volitional nuance of the perfect. The precative may be used in reference to situations the speaker prays for and expects to be realized, a prayer, or a request of confidence (e.g., 2 Sam 7:29; Job 21:16; 22:18; Pss 3:8; 4:2; 7:7; 22:22; 31:5-6; 71:3; Lam 1:21). See IBHS 494-95 ¬І30.5.4c, d. Most English versions employ a volitional nuance, whether precative or imperative of request (NRSV, NASB, NIV, TEV, NJPS, CEV). A few English versions adopt a prophetic perfect future-time nuance: вАЬthou wilt bring the day that thou hast calledвАЭ (KJV, ASV).
n25001130: tn Heb вАЬday.вАЭ The words вАЬof judgmentвАЭ were supplied in the translation. The term вАП„Щ„Х÷є„ЭвАО (yom, вАЬdayвАЭ) is often used as a metonymy of association, standing for the event associated with that particular time period: judgment (e.g., Isa 2:12; 13:6, 9; Jer 46:10; Lam 2:22; Ezek 13:5; 30:3; Amos 5:18, 20; Obad 15; Zeph 1:7, 14; Zech 14:1; Mal 4:5 [3:23 HT]) (BDB 399 s.v. 3).
n25001132: tn Heb вАЬand.вАЭ Following a volitive use of the perfect, the vav (вАП„ХвАО) prefixed to вАП„Х÷∞„Щ÷і„Ф÷∞„Щ„Х÷ЉвАО (veyihyu, вАЬand let it be!вАЭ) introduces a purpose/result clause in a dependent volitive construction: вАЬso that they may be like me!вАЭ
n25001134: tn For the nuance вАЬafflictвАЭ see the note at 1:12.
n25001135: tn The parallel statements вАЬafflict themвАЭ and вАЬjust as you have afflicted meвАЭ in the translation mirror the Hebrew wordplay between вАП„Ґ„Х÷є„Ь÷µ„Ь „Ь÷Є„Ю„Х÷євАО ( њolel lamo, вАЬMay you deal with themвАЭ) and вАП„Ґ„Х÷є„Ь÷Ј„Ь÷∞„™÷Љ÷Є „Ь÷і„ЩвАО ( њolalta li, вАЬyou dealt with meвАЭ).
n25001136: tn Heb вАЬall my rebellions,вАЭ that is, вАЬall my rebellious acts.вАЭ
n25001137: tn Heb вАЬis sorrowfulвАЭ or вАЬis faint.вАЭ The adjective вАП„У÷Ј„Х÷Љ÷Є„ЩвАО (davvay, вАЬfaintвАЭ) is used in reference to emotional sorrow (e.g., Isa 1:5; Lam 1:22; Jer 8:18). The cognate Aramaic term means вАЬsorrow,вАЭ and the cognate Syriac term refers to вАЬmiseryвАЭ (HALOT 216 s.v. *вАП„У÷Љ÷∞„Х÷Ј„ЩвАО). The related Hebrew adjective вАП„У÷Љ÷∞„Х÷Ј„ФвАО (devah) means вАЬ(physically) sickвАЭ and вАЬ(emotionally) sad,вАЭ while the related Hebrew verb вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„Х÷Є„ФвАО (davah) means вАЬto be sadвАЭ due to menstruation. The more literal English versions fail to bring out explicitly the nuance of emotional sorrow and create possible confusion as to whether the problem is simply loss of courage: вАЬmy heart is faintвАЭ (KJV, NKJV, RSV, NRSV, ASV, NASB, NIV). The more paraphrastic English versions explicate the emotional sorrow that this idiom connotes: вАЬmy heart is sickвАЭ (NJPS), вАЬI am sick at heartвАЭ (TEV), and вАЬIвАЩve lost all hope!вАЭ (CEV).
n250022: tc The MT reads вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) here rather than вАП„Щ„Ф„Х„ФвАО (YHWH, вАЬthe LordвАЭ). See the tc note at 1:14.
n250023: sn Chapter 2 continues the use of feminine epithets (e.g., вАЬDaughter ZionвАЭ) despite initially portraying Jerusalem as an object destroyed by the angered enemy, God.
n250024: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„Ґ÷і„Щ„СвАО (ya њiv) is a hapax legomenon (a term that appears only once in the Hebrew OT). Most lexicons take it as a denominative verb from the noun вАП„Ґ÷Є„СвАО ( њov, вАЬcloud,вАЭ HALOT 773 s.v. II вАП„Ґ÷Є„СвАО; BDB 728 s.v. вАП„Ґ„Х÷Љ„СвАО): Hiphil imperfect third person masculine singular from вАП„Ґ„Х÷є„СвАО (вАЩov), meaning вАЬcover with a cloud, make darkвАЭ (HALOT 794 s.v. вАП„Ґ„Х„СвАО) or вАЬbecloudвАЭ (BDB 728 s.v.): вАЬthe Lord has covered Daughter Zion with the cloud of His anger.вАЭ This approach is followed by many English versions (KJV, RSV, NASB, NIV). However, a few scholars relate it to a cognate Arabic verb denoting вАЬblame, revileвАЭ (Ehrlich, Rudolph, Hillers): вАЬthe Lord has shamed Daughter Zion in His anger.вАЭ Several English versions adopt this (NRSV, NJPS, CEV). The picture of cloud and wrath concurs with the stanzaвАЩs connection to вАЬday of the LordвАЭ imagery.
n250025: tn The common gloss for вАП„Ц÷Є„Ы÷Ј„®вАО (zakhar) is вАЬremember.вАЭ вАП„Ц÷Є„Ы÷Ј„®вАО (zakhar) entails вАЬbearing something in mindвАЭ in a broader sense than the English gloss вАЬremember.вАЭ When God вАЬbears someone in mind,вАЭ the consequences are beneficial for them. The implication of not regarding his footstool is to not esteem and so not care for or protect it.
n250026: tn Heb вАЬthe footstool of His feet.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ф÷≤„У÷є„ЭвАО (hadom, вАЬfootstoolвАЭ), always joined with вАП„®÷Ј„Т÷∞„Ь÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (raglayim, вАЬfeetвАЭ), is used figuratively in reference to the dwelling place of God (BDB 213 s.v. вАП„Ф÷≤„У÷є„ЭвАО), either of the LordвАЩs temple in Jerusalem (Isa 60:13; Lam 2:1) or of the ark as the place above which the Lord is enthroned (Pss 99:5; 132:7; 1 Chr 28:2). Once it refers to GodвАЩs enemies (Ps 110:1).
n250027: tn Heb вАЬin the day of His anger.вАЭ As a temporal reference this phrase means вАЬwhen he displayed his anger.вАЭ The Hebrew term вАЬday,вАЭ associated with the вАЬday of the LordвАЭ or вАЬday of his wrath,вАЭ also functions as a title in a technical sense.
n250028: tc The MT reads вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) here rather than вАП„Щ„Ф„Х„ФвАО (YHWH, вАЬthe LordвАЭ). See the tc note at 1:14.
n2500210: tc The Kethib is written вАП„Ь÷є„Р „Ч÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЬвАО (lo Њ khamal, вАЬwithout mercyвАЭ), while the Qere reads вАП„Х÷∞„Ь÷є„Р „Ч÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЬвАО (velo Њ khamal, вАЬand he has shown no mercyвАЭ). The Kethib is followed by the LXX, while the Qere is reflected in many Hebrew mss and the ancient versions (Syriac Peshitta, Aramaic Targum, Latin Vulgate). The English versions are split between the Kethib (вАЬThe Lord swallowed all the dwellings of Jacob without mercyвАЭ; cf. RSV, NRSV, NIV, TEV, NJPS) and the Qere (вАЬThe Lord swallowed all the dwellings of Jacob and has shown no mercyвАЭ; cf. KJV, NASB, CEV). As these words occur between a verb and its object (вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЬвАО [khamal] is not otherwise followed by вАП„Р÷µ„™вАО [ Њet, direct object marker]), an adverbial reading is the most natural, although interrupting the sentence with an insertion is possible. Cf. 2:17, 21; 3:43. In contexts of harming, to show mercy often means to spare from harm.
n2500213: tn Heb вАЬHe brought down to the ground in disgrace the kingdom and its princes.вАЭ The verbs вАП„Ч÷і„Ь÷Љ÷µ„ЬвАОвА¶вАП„Ф÷і„Т÷Љ÷і„Щ„ҐвАО (higgi њвА¶khillel, вАЬhe has brought downвА¶he has profanedвАЭ) function as a verbal hendiadys, as the absence of the conjunction вАП„ХвАО (vav) suggests. The first verb retains its full verbal force, while the second functions adverbially: вАЬhe has brought down [direct object] in disgrace.вАЭ
n2500214: tc The MT reads вАП„Р÷Ј„£вАО ( Њaf, вАЬangerвАЭ), while the ancient versions (LXX, Syriac Peshitta, Latin Vulgate) reflect вАП„Р÷Ј„§÷Љ„Х÷євАО ( Њappo, вАЬHis angerвАЭ). The MT is the more difficult reading syntactically, while the ancient versions are probably smoothing out the text.
n2500216: tn Heb вАЬevery horn of Israel.вАЭ The term вАЬhornвАЭ (вАП„І÷ґ„®÷ґ„ЯвАО, qeren) normally refers to the horn of a bull, one of the most powerful animals in ancient Israel. This term is often used figuratively as a symbol of strength, usually in reference to the military might of an army (Deut 33:17; 1 Sam 2:1, 10; 2 Sam 22:3; Pss 18:3; 75:11; 89:18, 25; 92:11; 112:9; 1 Chr 25:5; Jer 48:25; Lam 2:3, 17; Ezek 29:21) (BDB 901 s.v. 2), just as warriors are sometimes figuratively described as вАЬbulls.вАЭ Cutting off the вАЬhornвАЭ is a figurative expression for destroying warriors (Jer 48:25; Ps 75:10 [11 HT]).
n2500217: tn Heb вАЬhe caused his right hand to turn back.вАЭ The implication in such contexts is that the LordвАЩs right hand protects his city. This image of the right hand is consciously reversed in 2:4.
n2500218: tn Heb вАЬfrom the presence of the enemy.вАЭ This figurative expression refers to the approach of the attacking army.
n2500219: tn Heb вАЬhe burned in Jacob like a flaming fire.вАЭ
n2500220: tn Or вАЬHe burned against Jacob as a raging fire consumes all around.вАЭ
n2500221: tn Heb вАЬbent His bow.вАЭ When the verb вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (darakh) is used with the noun вАП„І÷ґ„©„Б÷ґ„™вАО (qeshet, вАЬarcher-bowвАЭ), it means вАЬto bend [a bow]вАЭ to string it in preparation for shooting arrows (1 Chr 5:18; 8:40; 2 Chr 14:7; Jer 50:14, 29; 51:3). This idiom is used figuratively to describe the assaults of the wicked (Pss 11:2; 37:14) and the judgments of the Lord (Ps 7:13; Lam 2:4; 3:12) (BDB 202 s.v. вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО 4). The translation вАЬhe prepared his bowвАЭ is the slightly more general modern English idiomatic equivalent of the ancient Hebrew idiom вАЬhe bent his bowвАЭвАФboth refer to preparations to get ready to shoot arrows.
n2500223: tn Heb вАЬthe ones who were pleasing to the eye.вАЭ
n2500224: tn The singular noun вАП„Р÷є„Ф÷ґ„ЬвАО ( Њohel, вАЬtentвАЭ) may function as a collective, referring to all tents in Judah. A parallel expression occurs in verse 2 using the plural: вАЬall the dwellings of JacobвАЭ (вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Њ„†÷∞„Р„Х÷є„™ „Щ÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„І÷є„СвАО, kol ne Њot ya њaqov). The singular вАЬtentвАЭ matches the image of вАЬDaughter Zion.вАЭ On the other hand, the singular вАЬthe tent of Daughter ZionвАЭ might be a hyperbolic synecdoche of container (= tent) for contents (= inhabitants of Zion).
n2500225: tc The MT reads вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) here rather than вАП„Щ„Ф„Х„ФвАО (YHWH, вАЬthe LordвАЭ). See the tc note at 1:14.
n2500228: tn Heb вАЬhis.вАЭ For consistency this has been translated as вАЬher.вАЭ
n2500229: tn Heb вАЬHe increased in Daughter Judah mourning and lamentation.вАЭ
n2500230: tn Heb вАЬHis booth.вАЭ The noun вАП„©„В„Ъ÷∞вАО (sokh, вАЬbooth,вАЭ BDB 968 s.v.) is a hapax legomenon (term that appears only once in the Hebrew OT). But it is probably an alternate spelling of the more common noun вАП„°÷ї„Ы÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (sukkah, вАЬboothвАЭ), which is used frequently of temporary shelters and booths (e.g., Neh 8:15) (BDB 697 s.v. вАП„°÷ї„Ы÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО). This is a figurative description of the temple, as the parallel term вАП„Ю„Х÷є„Ґ÷≤„У„Х÷євАО (mo њado, вАЬhis tabernacleвАЭ or вАЬhis appointed meeting placeвАЭ) makes clear. Jeremiah probably chose this term to emphasize the frailty of the temple and its ease of destruction. Contrary to the expectation of Jerusalem, it was only a temporary dwelling of the LordвАФits permanence cut short due to sin of the people.
n2500231: tc The MT reads вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Ј„Т÷Љ÷Ј„ЯвАО (kaggan, вАЬlike a gardenвАЭ). The LXX reads бљ°ѕВ бЉДќЉѕАќµќїќњќљ (h≈Нs ampelon), which reflects вАП„Ы÷Љ÷∞„Т÷ґ„§÷ґ„ЯвАО (kegefen, вАЬlike a vineyardвАЭ). Internal evidence favors вАП„Ы÷Љ÷∞„Т÷ґ„§÷ґ„ЯвАО (kegefen) because GodвАЩs judgment is often compared to the destruction of a vineyard (e.g., Job 15:33; Isa 34:4; Ezek 15:2, 6).
n2500232: tn Heb вАЬThe Lord has caused to be forgotten in Zion both appointed festival and Sabbath.вАЭ The verb вАП„©„Б÷і„Ы÷Љ÷Ј„ЧвАО (shikkakh, вАЬto make forgottenвАЭ), the only Piel form of вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЧвАО (shakhakh, вАЬto forgetвАЭ), is used figuratively. When people forget, вАЬoften the neglect of obligations is in viewвАЭ (L. C. Allen, NIDOTTE 4:104). When people forget the things of God, they are in disobedience and often are indicted for ignoring God or neglecting their duties to him (Deut 4:23, 31; 6:12; 8:11, 19; 26:13; 31:21; 32:18; Judg 3:7; 1 Sam 12:9; 2 Kgs 17:38; Is 49:14; 51:13; 65:11; Jer 18:15; Ezek 23:35; Hos 4:6). The irony is that the one to whom worship is due has made it so that people must neglect it. Most English versions render the verb in a metonymical sense: вАЬbrought to an endвАЭ (RSV), вАЬdid away withвАЭ (CEV), вАЬput an end toвАЭ (TEV), вАЬhas endedвАЭ (NJPS), вАЬhas abolishedвАЭ (NRSV). Few English versions employ the gloss вАЬforgetвАЭ: вАЬthe Lord hath caused the solemn feasts and sabbaths to be forgottenвАЭ (KJV), and вАЬthe Lord has made Zion forget her appointed feasts and her sabbathsвАЭ(NIV).
n2500233: tn Heb вАЬIn the fury of his angerвАЭ (вАП„Ц÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„Э÷Њ„Р„§÷Љ„Х÷євАО, za њam  Њappo). The genitive noun вАП„Р„§÷Љ„Х÷євАО ( Њappo, вАЬhis angerвАЭ) functions as an attributed genitive with the construct noun вАП„Ц÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„ЭвАО (za њam, вАЬfury, rageвАЭ): вАЬhis furious anger.вАЭ
n2500234: tn The verb вАП„†÷Є„Р÷Ј„•вАО (na Њats, вАЬto spurn, show contemptвАЭ) functions as a metonymy of cause (= to spurn king and priests) for effect (= to reject them; cf. CEV). Since spurning is the cause, this may be understood as вАЬto reject with a negative attitude.вАЭ However, retaining вАЬspurnвАЭ in the translation keeps the term emotionally loaded. The most frequent term for вАП„†÷Є„Р÷Ј„•вАО (na Њats) in the LXX (ѕАќ±ѕБќњќЊѕНќљѕЙ, paroxun≈Н) also conveys emotion beyond a decision to reject.
n2500235: tc The MT reads вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) here rather than вАП„Щ„Ф„Х„ФвАО (YHWH, вАЬthe LordвАЭ), which occurs near the end of this verse. See the tc note at 1:14.
n2500236: tn The Heb verb вАП„Ц÷Є„†÷Ј„ЧвАО (zanakh) is a rejection term often used in military contexts. Emphasizing emotion, it may mean вАЬto spurn.вАЭ In military contexts it may be rendered вАЬto desert.вАЭ
n2500237: tn Heb вАЬHis sanctuary.вАЭ The term вАП„Ю÷і„І÷∞„У÷Љ÷Є„©„Б„Х÷євАО (miqdasho, вАЬHis sanctuaryвАЭ) refers to the temple (e.g., 1 Chr 22:19; 2 Chr 36:17; Ps 74:7; Isa 63:18; Ezek 48:21; Dan 8:11) (BDB 874 s.v. вАП„Ю÷і„І÷∞„У÷Љ÷Є„©„БвАО).
n2500238: tn Heb вАЬHe delivered into the hand of the enemy.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ф÷і„°÷∞„Т÷Љ÷і„Щ„®вАО (hisgir), Hiphil perfect third person masculine singular from вАП„°÷Є„Т÷Ј„®вАО (sagar), means вАЬto give into someoneвАЩs control: to deliverвАЭ (Deut 23:16; Josh 20:5; 1 Sam 23:11, 20; 30:15; Job 16:11; Pss 31:9; 78:48, 50, 62; Lam 2:7; Amos 1:6, 9; Obad 14).
n2500240: tn Heb вАЬthey gave voiceвАЭ (вАП„І„Х÷є„Ь „†÷Є„™÷∞„†„Х÷ЉвАО, kol natenu). The verb вАП„†÷Є„™÷Ј„ЯвАО (natan, вАЬto giveвАЭ) with the noun вАП„І„Х÷є„ЬвАО (kol, вАЬvoice, soundвАЭ) is an idiom meaning: вАЬto utter a sound, make a noise, raise the voiceвАЭ (e.g., Gen 45:2; Prov 2:3; Jer 4:16; 22:20; 48:34) (HALOT 734 s.v. вАП„†„™„ЯвАО 12; BDB 679 s.v. вАП„†÷Є„™÷Ј„ЯвАО 1.x). Contextually, this describes the shout of victory by the Babylonians celebrating their conquest of Jerusalem.
n2500241: tn Heb вАЬas on the day of an appointed time.вАЭ The term вАП„Ю„Х÷є„Ґ÷µ„УвАО (mo њed, вАЬappointed timeвАЭ) refers to the religious festivals that were celebrated at appointed times in the Hebrew calendar (BDB 417 s.v. 1.b). In contrast to making festivals neglected (forgotten) in v 6, the enemy had a celebration that was entirely out of place.
n2500242: tn Heb вАЬhe stretched out a measuring line.вАЭ In Hebrew, this idiom is used (1) literally: to describe a workmanвАЩs preparation of measuring and marking stones before cutting them for building (Job 38:5; Jer 31:39; Zech 1:16), and (2) figuratively: to describe the LordвАЩs planning and preparation to destroy a walled city, that is, to mark off for destruction (2 Kgs 21:13; Isa 34:11; Lam 2:8). It is not completely clear how a phrase from the vocabulary of building becomes a metaphor for destruction; however, it might picture a predetermined and carefully planned measure from which God will not deviate.
n2500243: tn Heb вАЬHe did not return His hand from swallowing.вАЭ That is, he persisted until it was destroyed.
n2500244: tn Heb вАЬthey languished together.вАЭ The verbs вАП„Р÷Є„С÷Ј„ЬвАО ( Њaval, вАЬto lamentвАЭ) and вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЬвАО ( Њamal, вАЬlanguish, mournвАЭ) are often used in contexts of funeral laments in secular settings. The Hebrew prophets often use these terms to describe the aftermath of the LordвАЩs judgment on a nation. Based on parallel terms, вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЬвАО ( Њamal) may describe either mourning or deterioration and so makes for a convenient play on meaning when destroyed objects are personified. Incorporating this play into the translation, however, may obscure the parallel between this line and the deterioration of the gates beginning in v. 9.
n2500245: tn Heb вАЬhave sunk down.вАЭ This expression, вАЬher gates have sunk down into the ground,вАЭ is a personification picturing the city gates descending into the earth as if going down into the grave or the netherworld. Most English versions render it literally (KJV, RSV, NRSV, NASB, NIV, NJPS); however, a few paraphrases have captured the equivalent sense quite well: вАЬZionвАЩs gates have fallen facedown on the groundвАЭ (CEV), and вАЬthe gates are buried in rubbleвАЭ (TEV).
n2500246: tn Heb вАЬhe has destroyed and smashed her bars.вАЭ The two verbs вАП„Р÷і„С÷Љ÷Ј„У „Х÷∞„©„Б÷і„С÷Љ÷Ј„®вАО ( Њibbad veshibbar) form a verbal hendiadys that emphasizes the forcefulness of the destruction of the locking bars on the gates. The first verb functions adverbially, and the second retains its full verbal sense: вАЬhe has smashed to pieces.вАЭ Several English versions render this expression literally and miss the rhetorical point: вАЬhe has ruined and brokenвАЭ (RSV, NRSV), вАЬhe has destroyed and brokenвАЭ (KJV, NASB), and вАЬhe has broken and destroyedвАЭ (NIV). The hendiadys has been correctly noted by others: вАЬsmashed to piecesвАЭ (TEV, CEV) and вАЬsmashed to bitsвАЭ (NJPS).
n2500247: tn Heb вАЬher bars.вАЭ Since the literal вАЬbarsвАЭ could be misunderstood as referring to saloons, the phrase вАЬthe bars that lock her gatesвАЭ has been used in the present translation.
n2500249: tn Heb вАЬthere is no torah,вАЭ or вАЬthere is no TorahвАЭ (вАП„Р÷µ„Щ„Я „™÷Љ„Х÷є„®÷Є„ФвАО,  Њen torah). Depending on whether вАП„™÷Љ„Х÷є„®÷Є„ФвАО (torah, вАЬinstruction, lawвАЭ) is used in parallelism with the preceding or following line, it refers to (1) political guidance that the now-exiled king had formerly provided or (2) prophetic instruction that the now-ineffective prophets had formerly provided (BDB 434 s.v. вАП„™÷Љ„Х÷є„®÷Є„ФвАО 1.b). It is plausible that the three lines are arranged in an ABA chiastic structure, exploiting the semantic ambiguity of the term вАП„™÷Љ„Х÷є„®÷Є„ФвАО (torah, вАЬinstructionвАЭ). Conceivably it is an oblique reference to the priestsвАЩ duties of teaching, thus introducing a third group of the countries leaders. It is possible to hear in this a lament in reference to the destruction of Torah scrolls that may have been at the temple when it was destroyed.
n2500251: tc Consonantal вАП„Щ„©„Б„С„ХвАО (yshvy) is vocalized by the MT as вАП„Щ÷µ„©„Б÷∞„С„Х÷ЉвАО (yeshevu), Qal imperfect third person masculine plural from вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„СвАО (yashav, вАЬto sitвАЭ): вАЬthey sit on the ground.вАЭ However, the ancient versions (Aramaic Targum, Greek Septuagint, Syriac Peshitta, Latin Vulgate) reflect a Qal perfect vocalization: вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷∞„С„Х÷ЉвАО (yashevu, вАЬthey have sat [down]вАЭ).
n2500252: tn Heb вАЬthey sit on the ground; they are silent.вАЭ Based on meter, the two verbs вАП„Щ÷і„У÷Љ÷∞„Ю„Х÷ЉвАОвА¶вАП„Щ÷µ„©„Б÷∞„С„Х÷ЉвАО (yeshevuвА¶yiddemu, вАЬthey sitвА¶they are silentвАЭ) are in the same half of the line. Joined without a вАП„ХвАО (vav) conjunction they form a verbal hendiadys. The first functions in its full verbal sense while the second functions adverbially: вАЬthey sit in silence.вАЭ The verb вАП„Щ÷і„У÷Љ÷∞„Ю„Х÷ЉвАО (yiddemu) may mean to be silent or to wail.
n2500253:
tn Heb вАЬthey have girded themselves with sackcloth.вАЭ
sn Along with putting dirt on oneвАЩs head, wearing sackcloth was a sign of mourning.
n2500254: tn Heb вАЬthe virgins of Jerusalem.вАЭ The term вАЬvirginsвАЭ is a metonymy of association, standing for single young women who are not yet married. These single women are in grief because their potential suitors have been killed. The elders, old men, and young women function together as a merism for all of the survivors (F. W. Dobbs-Allsopp, Lamentations [IBC], 92).
n2500255: tn Heb вАЬhave bowed down their heads to the ground.вАЭ
n2500256: tn Heb вАЬmy eyes are spent,вАЭ or вАЬmy eyes fail.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (kalah) is used of eyes exhausted by weeping (Job 11:20; 17:5; Ps 69:4; Jer 14:6; 4:17), and means either вАЬto be spentвАЭ (BDB 477 s.v. 2.b) or вАЬto failвАЭ (HALOT 477 s.v. 6). It means to have used up all oneвАЩs tears or to have worn out the eyes because of so much crying. It is rendered variously: вАЬmy eyes failвАЭ (KJV, NIV), вАЬmy eyes are spentвАЭ (RSV, NRSV, NASB, NJPS), вАЬmy eyes are worn outвАЭ (TEV), and вАЬmy eyes are redвАЭ (CEV).
n2500257: tn Heb вАЬbecause of tears.вАЭ The plural noun вАП„У÷Љ÷і„Ю÷∞„Ґ„Х÷є„™вАО (dim њot, вАЬtearsвАЭ) is an example of the plural of intensity or repeated behavior: вАЬmany tears.вАЭ The more common singular form вАП„У÷Љ÷і„Ю÷∞„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО (dim њah) normally functions in a collective sense (вАЬtearsвАЭ); therefore, the plural form here does not indicate simple plural of number.
n2500258: tn Heb вАЬmy bowels burn,вАЭ or вАЬmy bowels are in a ferment.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ч÷≥„Ю÷Ј„®÷∞„Ю÷∞„®„Х÷ЉвАО (khomarmeru) is an unusual form that is derived from a debated root: a Poalal from III вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю÷Ј„®вАО (khamar, вАЬto be red,вАЭ HALOT 330 s.v. III вАП„Ч„Ю„®вАО) or a Pe њal њal from I вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю÷Ј„®вАО (khamar, вАЬto ferment, boil up,вАЭ BDB 330 s.v. I вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю÷Ј„®вАО). The Poalal stem of this verb occurs only three times in OT: with вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„†÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (panim, вАЬface,вАЭ Job 16:16) and вАП„Ю÷µ„Ґ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (me њim, вАЬbowels,вАЭ Lam 1:20; 2:11). The phrase вАП„Ч÷≥„Ю÷Ј„®÷∞„Ю÷∞„®„Х÷Љ „Ю÷µ„Ґ÷Ј„ЩвАО (khomarmeru me њay) means вАЬmy bowels burnedвАЭ (HALOT 330 s.v.), or вАЬmy bowels are in a ferment,вАЭ as a euphemism for lower-intestinal bowel problems (BDB 330 s.v.). This phrase also occurs in later rabbinic literature (m. Sanhedrin 7:2). The present translation, вАЬmy stomach is in knots,вАЭ is not a literal equivalent to this Hebrew idiom; however, it is an attempt to approximate the equivalent English idiom.
n2500259: tn Heb вАЬmy liver,вАЭ viewed as the seat of the emotions.
n2500261: tn Heb вАЬthe daughter of my people.вАЭ Rather than a genitive of relationship (вАЬdaughter of XвАЭ), the phrase вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„™÷Њ„Ґ÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (bat  њammi) is probably a genitive of apposition. The idiom вАЬDaughter XвАЭ occurs often in Lamentations: вАЬDaughter JerusalemвАЭ (2x), вАЬDaughter ZionвАЭ (7x), вАЬVirgin Daughter ZionвАЭ (1x), вАЬDaughter of My PeopleвАЭ (5x), вАЬDaughter JudahвАЭ (2x), and вАЬVirgin Daughter JudahвАЭ (1x). In each case, it is a poetic description of Jerusalem or Judah as a whole. The idiom вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„™÷Њ„Ґ÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (bat  њammi, lit., вАЬdaughter of my peopleвАЭ is rendered variously by the English versions: вАЬthe daughter of my peopleвАЭ (KJV, RSV, NASB), вАЬmy peopleвАЭ (NIV, TEV, CEV), and вАЬmy poor peopleвАЭ (NJPS). The metaphor here pictures the people as vulnerable and weak.
n2500262: tn Heb вАЬtheyвАЭ; the referent has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2500263: tn Heb вАЬto their mother,вАЭ understood as a collective singular.
n2500264: tn Heb вАЬWhere is bread and wine?вАЭ The terms вАЬbreadвАЭ and вАЬwineвАЭ are synecdoches of specific (= bread, wine) for general (= food, drink).
n2500265: tn Heb вАЬas they faint,вАЭ or вАЬwhen they faint.вАЭ
n2500266: tn Heb вАЬas their life is poured out.вАЭ The term вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ф÷і„©„Б÷∞„™÷Љ÷Ј„§÷Љ÷µ„Ъ÷∞вАО (behishtappekh), Hitpael infinitive construct + the preposition вАП„С÷ЉвАО (bet), from вАП„©„Б÷Є„§÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (shafakh, вАЬto pour outвАЭ), may be rendered вАЬas they expireвАЭ (BDB 1050 s.v. вАП„©„Б÷Є„§÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО), referring to the process of dying. Note the repetition of the word вАЬpour outвАЭ with various direct objects in this poem at 2:4, 11, 12, and 19.
