n2500268: tc The MT reads вАП„Р÷≤„Ґ÷і„Щ„У÷µ„Ъ÷∞вАО ( Њa њidekh), Hiphil imperfect first person common singular + second person feminine singular suffix from вАП„Ґ÷Є„У÷Є„ФвАО ( њadah, вАЬto testifyвАЭ): вАЬ[How] can I testify for you?вАЭ However, Latin Vulgate comparabo te reflects the reading вАП„Р÷ґ„Ґ÷±„®÷Є„Ъ÷∞вАО ( Њe њerakh), Qal imperfect first person common singular from вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО ( њarakh, вАЬto likenвАЭ): вАЬ[To what] can I liken [you]?вАЭ The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО ( њarakh) normally means вАЬto lay out, set in rows; to get ready, set in order; to line up for battle, set battle formation,вАЭ but it also may denote вАЬto compare (as a result of arranging in order), to make equalвАЭ (e.g., Pss 40:6; 89:6 [7 HT]; Job 28:17, 19; Isa 40:18; 44:7). The BHS editors suggest the emendation, which involves simple orthographic confusion between вАП„®вАО (resh) and вАП„УвАО (dalet), and deletion of вАП„ЩвАО (yod), which the MT could have added to make sense of the form. The variant is favored based on internal evidence: (1) it is the more difficult reading because the meaning вАЬto compareвАЭ for вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО ( њarakh) is less common than вАП„Ґ÷Є„У÷Є„ФвАО ( њadah, вАЬto testifyвАЭ), (2) it recovers a tight parallelism between вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО ( њarakh, вАЬto likenвАЭ) and вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (damah, вАЬto compareвАЭ) (e.g., Ps 89:6 [7 HT]; Isa 40:18), and (3) the MT reading, вАЬHow can I testify for you?вАЭ makes little sense in the context. Nevertheless, most English versions hold to the MT reading: KJV, RSV, NRSV, NASB, NIV, TEV, and CEV. This textual emendation was first proposed by J. Meinhold, вАЬThreni 2, 13, вАЭ ZAW 15 (1895): 286.
n2500269: tc The MT reads вАП„Ю÷Є„Ф „Р÷Ј„©„Б÷∞„Х÷ґ„Ф÷Њ„Ь÷Љ÷Є„Ъ÷∞ „Х÷Ј„Р÷≤„†÷Ј„Ч÷≤„Ю÷µ„Ъ÷∞вАО (mah  Њashveh lakh va Њanakhamekh, вАЬTo what can I compare you so that I might comfort you?вАЭ). The LXX reflects a Vorlage of вАП„Ю÷і„Щ „Щ„Х÷є„©„Б÷і„Щ„Ґ÷Ј „Ь÷Є„Ъ÷∞ „Х÷∞„†÷і„Ч÷Ј„Ю÷∞„Ъ÷ЄвАО (mi yoshia њ lakh venikhamekha, вАЬWho will save you so that he might comfort you?вАЭ). This textual variant reflects several cases of orthographic confusion between similarly spelled words. The MT best explains the origin of the LXX textual variants. Internal evidence of contextual congruence favors the MT as the original reading.
n2500270: tn The вАП„ХвАО (vav) prefixed to вАП„Х÷Ј„Р÷≤„†÷Ј„Ч÷≤„Ю÷µ„Ъ÷∞вАО (va Њanakhamekh, вАЬI might comfort youвАЭ) denotes purpose: вАЬso thatвА¶.вАЭ
n2500272: tc The MT reads вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Щ÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО (kayyam, вАЬas the seaвАЭ), while the LXX reflects a Vorlage of вАП„Ы÷Љ„Х÷є„°вАО (kos, вАЬa cupвАЭ). The textual variant is probably due to simple orthographic confusion between letters of similar appearance. The idiomatic expression favors the MT.
n2500273: sn The rhetorical question implies a denial: вАЬNo one can heal you!вАЭ The following verses, 14-17, present four potential healersвАФprophets, passersby, enemies, and God.
n2500274: tn Heb вАЬworthless and whitewash.вАЭ The words вАП„©„Б÷Є„Х÷∞„Р „Х÷∞„™÷Є„§÷µ„ЬвАО (shav Њ vetafel) form a nominal hendiadys, meaning вАЬworthless whitewashвАЭ or вАЬworthless deceptions.вАЭ The noun вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„§÷µ„ЬвАО (tafel, вАЬwhitewashвАЭ) is used literally in reference to a white-washed wall (Ezek 13:10, 11, 14, 15) and figuratively in reference to false prophets (Ezek 22:28).
n2500275: tc The Kethib вАП„©„Б÷∞„С÷і„Щ„™÷µ„Ъ÷∞вАО (shevitekh) and the Qere вАП„©„Б÷∞„С„Х÷Љ„™÷µ„Ъ÷∞вАО (shevutekh), which is preserved in many medieval Hebrew mss here and elsewhere (Ps 85:1 [85:2 HT]; 126:4; Job 42:10), are struggling with the root. The ancient versions take it from вАП„©„Б÷Є„С÷Є„ФвАО (shavah), meaning вАЬcaptivity.вАЭ Such a meaning is not tenable for the Job passage, which suggests, along with a similar phrase in the Sefire inscription, that the proper meaning is вАЬto restore someoneвАЩs fortunes.вАЭ See HALOT 1386 s.v. вАП„©„Б÷∞„С„Х÷Љ„™вАО.
n2500276: tn Heb вАЬworthless and enticements.вАЭ The words вАП„©„Б÷Є„Х÷∞„Р „Х÷Љ„Ю÷Ј„У÷Љ„Х÷Љ„Ч÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (shav Њ umaddukhim) form a nominal hendiadys meaning вАЬworthless enticementsвАЭ or вАЬmisleading falsehoods.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„У÷Љ„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (madduakh), meaning вАЬenticementвАЭ or вАЬtransgression,вАЭ is a hapax legomenon (term that appears only once in the Hebrew OT). It is related to the verb вАП„†÷Є„У÷Є„ЧвАО (nadakh, вАЬto entice, lead astrayвАЭ), which often refers to idolatry.
n2500277: tn Heb вАЬclap their hands at you.вАЭ Clapping hands at someone was an expression of malicious glee, derision, and mockery (Num 24:10; Job 27:23; Lam 2:15).
n2500279: tn Heb вАЬperfection of beauty.вАЭ The noun вАП„Щ÷є„§÷і„ЩвАО (yofi, вАЬbeautyвАЭ) functions as a genitive of respect in relation to the preceding construct noun: Jerusalem was perfect in respect to its physical beauty.
n2500280: tn Heb вАЬthe joy of all the earth.вАЭ This is similar to statements found in Pss 48:2 and 50:2.
n2500281: tn Heb вАЬthey have opened wide their mouth against you.вАЭ
n2500283: tn Heb вАЬWe have attained; we have seen!вАЭ The verbs вАП„Ю÷Є„¶÷Є„Р„†„Х÷Љ „®÷Є„Р÷і„Щ„†„Х÷ЉвАО (matsa Њnu ra Њinu) form a verbal hendiadys in which the first retains its full verbal sense and the second functions as an object complement. It forms a Hebrew idiom that means something like, вАЬWe have lived to see it!вАЭ The three asyndetic first person common plural statements in 2:16 (вАЬWe waited; we destroyed; we saw!вАЭ) are spoken in an impassioned, staccato style reflecting the delight of the conquerors.
n2500284: tn The verb вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„¶÷Ј„ҐвАО (batsa њ) has a broad range of meanings: (1) вАЬto cut off, break off,вАЭ (2) вАЬto injureвАЭ a person, (3) вАЬto gain by violence,вАЭ (4) вАЬto finish, complete,вАЭ and (5) вАЬto accomplish, fulfillвАЭ a promise.
n2500285: tn Heb вАЬHis word.вАЭ When used in collocation with the verb вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„¶÷Ј„ҐвАО (batsa њ, вАЬto fulfill,вАЭ see previous tn), the accusative noun вАП„Р÷і„Ю÷∞„®÷Є„ФвАО ( Њimrah, вАЬwordвАЭ) means вАЬpromise.вАЭ
n2500286: tn Heb вАЬcommandedвАЭ or вАЬdecreed.вАЭ If a reference to prophetic oracles is understood, then вАЬdecreedвАЭ is preferable. If understood as a reference to the warnings in the covenant, then вАЬthreatenedвАЭ is a preferable rendering.
n2500288: tn Heb вАЬHe has overthrown and has not shown mercy.вАЭ The two verbs вАП„Ч÷Є„®÷Ј„° „Х÷∞„Ь÷є„Р „Ч÷Є„Ю÷Є„ЬвАО (kharas velo Њ khamal) form a verbal hendiadys in which the first retains its verbal sense and the second functions adverbially: вАЬHe has overthrown you without mercy.вАЭ вАП„Х÷∞„Ь÷є„Р „Ч÷Є„Ю÷Є„ЬвАО (velo Њ khamal) alludes to 2:2.
n2500289: tn Heb вАЬHe has exalted the horn of your adversaries.вАЭ The term вАЬhornвАЭ (вАП„І÷ґ„®÷ґ„ЯвАО, qeren) normally refers to the horn of a bull, one of the most powerful animals in ancient Israel. This term is often used figuratively as a symbol of strength, usually in reference to the military might of an army (Deut 33:17; 1 Sam 2:1, 10; 2 Sam 22:3; Pss 18:3 HT [18:2 ET]; 75:11 HT [75:10 ET]; 89:18, 25 HT [89:17, 24 ET]; 92:11 HT [92:10 ET]; 112:9; 1 Chr 25:5; Jer 48:25; Lam 2:3; Ezek 29:21), just as warriors are sometimes figuratively described as вАЬbulls.вАЭ To lift up the horn often means to boast, and to lift up someone elseвАЩs horn is to give victory or cause to boast.
n2500290: tc The MT reads вАП„¶÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„І „Ь÷і„С÷Љ÷Є„Э „Р÷µ„Ь÷Њ„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО (tsa њaq libbam  Њel  Њadonay, вАЬtheir heart cried out to the LordвАЭ), which neither matches the second person address characterizing 2:13-19 nor is in close parallel to the rest of verse 18. Since the perfect вАП„¶÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ІвАО (tsa њaq, вАЬcry outвАЭ) is apparently parallel to imperatives, it could be understood as a precative (вАЬlet their heart cry outвАЭ), although this understanding still has the problem of being in the third person. The BHS editors and many text critics suggest emending the MT вАП„¶÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ІвАО (tsa њaq) to вАП„¶÷Є„Ґ÷≤„І÷і„ЩвАО (tsa њaqi), Qal imperative second person feminine singular: вАЬCry out!вАЭ This restores a tighter parallelism with the two second person feminine singular imperatives introducing the following lines: вАП„Ф„Х÷є„®÷і„Щ„У÷і„ЩвАО (horidi, вАЬLet [your tears] flow down!вАЭ) and вАП„Р÷Ј„Ь÷Њ„™÷Љ÷і„™÷Љ÷∞„†÷і„ЩвАО ( Њal titteni, вАЬDo not allow!вАЭ). In such a case, вАП„Ь÷і„С÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО (libbam) must be taken adverbially. For вАП„Ь÷і„С÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО (libbam, вАЬtheir heartвАЭ), see the following note. The adverbial translation loses a potential parallel to the mention of the heart in the next verse. Emending the noun to вАЬyour heartвАЭ would maintain this connection.
n2500291: tn Heb вАЬtheir heartвАЭ or вАЬfrom the heart.вАЭ Many English versions take the вАП„ЭвАО (mem) on вАП„Ь÷і„С÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО (libbam) as the third person masculine plural pronominal suffix: вАЬtheir heartвАЭ (cf. KJV, NASB, NIV, NJPS, CEV). However, others take it as an enclitic or adverbial ending: вАЬfrom the heartвАЭ (cf. RSV, NRSV, TEV, NJPS margin). See T. F. McDaniel, вАЬThe Alleged Sumerian Influence upon Lamentations,вАЭ VT 18 (1968): 203-4.
n2500292: tc The MT reads вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) here rather than вАП„Щ„Ф„Х„ФвАО (YHWH, вАЬthe LordвАЭ). See the tc note at 1:14.
n2500293: tn The wall is a synecdoche of a part standing for the whole city.
n2500294: tn Heb вАЬday and night.вАЭ The expression вАЬday and nightвАЭ forms a merism which encompasses everything in between two polar opposites: вАЬfrom dawn to duskвАЭ or вАЬall day and all night long.вАЭ
n2500295: tn Heb вАЬthe daughter of your eye.вАЭ The term вАЬeyeвАЭ functions as a metonymy for вАЬtearsвАЭ that are produced by the eyes. Jeremiah exhorts personified Jerusalem to cry out to the Lord day and night without ceasing in repentance and genuine sorrow for its sins.
n2500296: tn Heb вАЬat the head of the watches.вАЭ
n2500297: tn The noun вАП„Ь÷µ„С÷Є„СвАО (levav, вАЬheartвАЭ) functions here as a metonymy of association for the thoughts and emotions in the heart. The Hebrew вАП„Ь÷µ„С÷Є„СвАО (levav) includes the mind, so in some cases the translation вАЬheartвАЭ implies an inappropriate division between the cognitive and affective. This context is certainly emotionally loaded, but as part of a series of admonitions to address God in prayer, these emotions are inextricably bound with the thoughts of the mind. The singular вАЬheartвАЭ is retained in the translation to be consistent with the personification of Jerusalem (cf. v. 18).
n2500298: tc The MT reads вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) here rather than вАП„Щ„Ф„Х„ФвАО (YHWH, вАЬthe LordвАЭ). See the tc note at 1:14.
n2500299: sn Lifting up the palms or hands is a metaphor for prayer.
n25002100: tn Heb вАЬon account of the life of your children.вАЭ The noun вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh) refers to the вАЬlifeвАЭ of their dying children (e.g., Lam 2:12). The singular noun вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh, вАЬlifeвАЭ) is used as a collective, as the plural genitive noun that follows makes clear: вАЬyour children.вАЭ
n25002101:
tc The BHS editors and many commentators suggest that the fourth bicolon in 2:19 is a late addition and should be deleted. Apart from the four sets of bicola in 1:7 and 2:19, every stanza in chapters 1-4 consists of three sets of bicola.
tn Heb вАЬwho are fainting.вАЭ
n25002102: tn Heb вАЬat the head of every street.вАЭ
n25002103:
tn Heb вАЬLook, O Lord! See!вАЭ When used in collocation with verbs of cognition, вАП„®÷Є„Р÷Є„ФвАО (ra Њah) means вАЬto see for oneselfвАЭ or вАЬto take noticeвАЭ (1 Sam 26:12). The parallelism between seeing and understanding is often emphasized (e.g., Exod 16:6; Isa 5:19; 29:15; Job 11:11; Eccl 6:5). See also 1:11 and compare 1:9, 12, 20; 3:50, 59, 60; 5:1.
sn Integral to battered JerusalemвАЩs appeal, and part of the ancient Near-Eastern lament genre, is the request for God to look at her pain. This should evoke pity regardless of the reason for punishment. The request is not for God to see merely that there are misfortunes, as one might note items on a checklist. The cognitive (facts) and affective (feelings) are not divided. The plea is for God to watch, think about, and be affected by these facts while listening to the petitionerвАЩs perspective.
n25002104: tn For the nuance вАЬafflictвАЭ see the note at 1:12.
n25002105: tn Heb вАЬtheir fruit.вАЭ The term вАП„§÷Љ÷∞„®÷і„ЩвАО (peri, вАЬfruitвАЭ) is used figuratively to refer to children as the fruit of a motherвАЩs womb (e.g., Gen 30:2; Deut 7:13; 28:4, 11, 18, 53; 30:9; Pss 21:11; 127:3; 132:11; Isa 13:18; Mic 6:7).
n25002106:
tn Heb вАЬinfants of healthy childbirth.вАЭ The genitive-construct phrase вАП„Ґ÷є„Ь÷≤„Ь÷µ„Щ „Ш÷і„§÷Љ÷ї„Ч÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( њolale tippukhim) functions as an attributive genitive construction: вАЬhealthy newborn infants.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ш÷і„§÷Љ÷ї„Ч÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (tippukhim) appears only here. It is related to the verb вАП„Ш÷Є„§÷Ј„ЧвАО (tafakh), meaning вАЬto give birth to a healthy childвАЭ or вАЬto raise childrenвАЭ depending on whether the Arabic or Akkadian cognate is emphasized. For the related verb, see below at 2:22.
sn Placing the specific reference to children at the end of the line in apposition to clarify that it does not describe the normal eating of fruit helps produce the repulsive shock of the image. Furthermore, the root of the word for вАЬinfantsвАЭ (вАП„Ґ„Х÷є„Ь÷µ„ЬвАО,  њolel) has the same root letters for the verb вАЬto afflictвАЭ occurring in the first line of the verse, making a pun (F. W. Dobbs-Allsopp, Lamentations [IBC], 99-100).
n25002107: tc The MT reads вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) here rather than вАП„Щ„Ф„Х„ФвАО (YHWH, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) as at the beginning of the verse. See the tc note at 1:14.
n25002108: tn Heb вАЬvirgins.вАЭ The term вАЬvirginвАЭ probably functions as a metonymy of association for single young women.
n25002109: tn Heb вАЬin the day of your anger.вАЭ The construction вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Щ„Х÷є„ЭвАО (bayom, вАЬin the day ofвА¶вАЭ) is a common Hebrew idiom, meaning вАЬwhenвА¶вАЭ (e.g., Gen 2:4; Lev 7:35; Num 3:1; Deut 4:15; 2 Sam 22:1; Pss 18:1; 138:3; Zech 8:9). This temporal idiom refers to a general time period but uses the term вАЬdayвАЭ as a forceful rhetorical device to emphasize the vividness and drama of the event, depicting it as occurring within a single day. In the ancient Near East, military-minded kings often referred to a successful campaign as вАЬthe day of XвАЭ in order to portray themselves as powerful conquerors who, as it were, could inaugurate and complete a victorious military campaign within the span of one day.
n25002110: tc The MT reads вАП„Ь÷є„Р „Ч÷Є„Ю÷Є„Ь÷∞„™÷Љ÷ЄвАО (lo Њ khamalta, вАЬYou showed no mercyвАЭ). However, many medieval Hebrew mss and most of the ancient versions (Aramaic Targum, Syriac Peshitta and Latin Vulgate) read вАП„Х÷∞„Ь÷є„Р „Ч÷Є„Ю÷Є„Ь÷∞„™÷Љ÷ЄвАО (velo Њ khamalta, вАЬand You showed no mercyвАЭ).
n25002111: tn The syntax of the line is awkward. English versions vary considerably in how they render it: вАЬThou hast called as in a solemn day my terrors round aboutвАЭ (KJV); вАЬThou hast called, as in the day of a solemn assembly, my terrors on every sideвАЭ (ASV); вАЬYou did call as in the day of an appointed feast my terrors on every sideвАЭ (NASB); вАЬThou didst invite as to the day of an appointed feast my terrors on every sideвАЭ (RSV); вАЬAs you summon to a feast day, so you summoned against me terrors on every sideвАЭ (NIV); вАЬYou summoned, as on a festival, my neighbors from roundaboutвАЭ (NJPS); вАЬYou invited my enemies to hold a carnival of terror all around meвАЭ (TEV); and вАЬYou invited my enemies like guests for a partyвАЭ (CEV).
n25002112: tn The term вАЬenemiesвАЭ is supplied in the translation as a clarification.
n25002113: tn Heb вАЬmy terrorsвАЭ or вАЬmy enemies.вАЭ The expression вАП„Ю÷∞„Т„Х÷Љ„®÷Ј„ЩвАО (meguray, вАЬmy terrorsвАЭ) is difficult and may refer to either enemies, the terror associated with facing enemies, or both.
n25002115: tn The meaning of the verb вАП„Ш÷Є„§÷Ј„ЧвАО (tafakh) is debated. The BDB lexicon suggests that it is derived from вАП„Ш÷Є„§÷Ј„ФвАО (tafah, вАЬto extend, spreadвАЭ the hands) and here means вАЬto carry in the palm of oneвАЩs handsвАЭ (BDB 381 s.v. вАП„Ш÷Є„§÷Ј„ФвАО 2), but HALOT 378 s.v. II вАП„Ш÷Є„§÷Ј„ЧвАО suggests that it is derived from the root II вАП„Ш÷Є„§÷Ј„ЧвАО (tafakh) and means вАЬto give birth to healthy children.вАЭ The recent lexicons hold that it is related to Arabic tafaha (вАЬto bring forth fully formed childrenвАЭ) and Akkadian tuppu (вАЬto raise childrenвАЭ). The use of this particular term highlights the tragic irony of what the army of Babylon has done: it has destroyed the lives of perfectly healthy children whom the women of Israel had raised.
n25002116: tn This entire line is an accusative noun clause, functioning as the direct object of the following line: вАЬmy enemy has destroyed the perfectly healthy childrenвА¶.вАЭ Normal word order in Hebrew is: verb + subject + direct object. Here, the accusative direct-object clause is moved forward for rhetorical emphasis: those whom the Babylonians killed had been children born perfectly healthy and then well raisedвА¶what a tragic loss of perfectly good human life!
n250031: sn The nature of the acrostic changes here. Each of the three lines in each verse, not just the first, begins with the corresponding letter of the alphabet.
n250032: tn The noun вАП„Т÷Љ÷ґ„С÷ґ„®вАО (gever, вАЬmanвАЭ) refers to a strong man, distinguished from women, children, and other non-combatants whom he is to defend. According to W. F. Lanahan the speaking voice in this chapter is that of a defeated soldier (вАЬThe Speaking Voice in the Book of LamentationsвАЭ JBL 93 [1974]: 41-49.) F. W. Dobbs-Allsopp (Lamentations [IBC], 108) argues that is the voice of an вАЬeveryman,вАЭ although вАЬone might not unreasonably suppose that some archetypal communal figure like the king does in fact stand in the distant background.вАЭ
n250033: tn The verb вАП„®÷Є„Р÷Є„ФвАО (ra Њah, вАЬto seeвАЭ) has a broad range of meanings, including (1) вАЬto seeвАЭ as to learn from experience and (2) вАЬto seeвАЭ as to experience (e.g., Gen 20:10; Ps 89:49; Eccl 5:17; Jer 5:12; 14:13; 20:18; 42:14; Zeph 3:15). Here it means that the speaker has experienced these things. The same Hebrew verb occurs in 2:20, where the Lord is asked to вАЬseeвАЭ (translated вАЬConsider!вАЭ), although it is difficult to maintain this connection in an English translation.
n250034: tn The noun вАП„©„Б÷µ„С÷ґ„ШвАО (shevet, вАЬrodвАЭ) refers to the weapon used for smiting an enemy (Exod 21:20; 2 Sam 23:21; 1 Chr 11:3; Isa 10:15; Mic 4:14 HT [5:1 ET]) and to the instrument of child-discipline (Prov 10:13; 22:15; 29:15). It is used figuratively to describe discipline of the individual (Job 9:34; 21:9; 37:13; 2 Sam 7:14; Ps 89:33) and the nation (Isa 10:5, 24; 14:29; 30:31).
n250035: tn The verb вАП„†÷Є„Ф÷Ј„ТвАО (nahag) describes the process of directing (usually a group of) something along a route, hence commonly вАЬto drive,вАЭ when describing flocks, caravans, or prisoners and spoils of war (1 Sam 23:5; 30:2). But with people it may also have a positive connotation вАЬto shepherdвАЭ or вАЬto guideвАЭ (Pss 48:14; 80:1). The line plays on this through the reversal of expectations. Rather than being safely shepherded by the Lord their king, he has driven them away into captivity.
n250036: tn The Hiphil of вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (halakh, вАЬto walkвАЭ) may be nuanced either as вАЬbroughtвАЭ (BDB 236 s.v. 1) or вАЬcaused to walkвАЭ (BDB 237 s.v. 5.a).
n250037: tn The two verbs вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷ї„С „Щ÷Ј„Ф÷≤„§÷є„Ъ÷∞вАО (yashuv yahafokh, вАЬhe returns, he turnsвАЭ) form a verbal hendiadys with the second verb retaining its full verbal sense while the first functions adverbially: вАЬhe repeatedly turnsвА¶вАЭ The verb вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„СвАО (shuv, lit., вАЬto returnвАЭ) functions adverbially to denote repetition: вАЬto do repeatedly, do again and againвАЭ (GKC 386-87 ¬І120.d, g) (Gen 26:18; 30:31; Num 11:4; Judg 19:7; 1 Sam 3:5, 6; 1 Kgs 13:33; 19:6; 21:3; 2 Chr 33:3; Job 10:16; 17:10; Ps 7:13; Jer 18:4; 36:28; Lam 3:3; Dan 9:25; Zech 5:1; 6:1; Mal 1:4).
n250038: tn The idiom вАЬto turn the hand againstвАЭ someone is a figurative expression denoting hostility. The term вАЬhandвАЭ (вАП„Щ÷Є„УвАО, yad) is often used in idioms denoting hostility (Exod 9:3, 15; Deut 2:15; Judg 2:15; 1 Sam 5:3, 6, 9; 6:9; 2 Sam 24:16; 2 Chr 30:12; Ezra 7:9; Job 19:21; Ps 109:27; Jer 15:17; 16:21; Ezek 3:14). The reference to GodвАЩs вАЬhandвАЭ is anthropomorphic.
n250039: tn Heb вАЬall of the day.вАЭ The idiom вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Њ„Ф÷Ј„Щ÷Љ„Х÷є„ЭвАО (kol hayom, вАЬall dayвАЭ) means вАЬcontinuallyвАЭ or вАЬall day longвАЭ (Gen 6:5; Deut 28:32; 33:12; Pss 25:5; 32:3; 35:28; 37:26; 38:7, 13; 42:4, 11; 44:9, 16, 23; 52:3; 56:2, 3, 6; 71:8, 15, 24; 72:15; 73:14; 74:22; 86:3; 88:18; 89:17; 102:9; 119:97; Prov 21:26; 23:17; Isa 28:24; 51:13; 52:5; 65:2, 5; Jer 20:7, 8; Lam 1:13; 3:14, 62; Hos 12:2).
n2500310: tn Heb вАЬmy flesh and my skin.вАЭ The two nouns joined with вАП„ХвАО (vav), вАП„С÷∞„©„В÷Є„®÷і„Щ „Х÷∞„Ґ„Х÷є„®÷і„ЩвАО (vesari ve њori, вАЬmy flesh and my skinвАЭ), form a nominal hendiadys. The first functions adjectivally, and the second retains its full nominal sense: вАЬmy mortal skin.вАЭ
n2500311: tn Heb вАЬhe has built against me.вАЭ The verb вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„†÷Є„ФвАО (banah, вАЬto buildвАЭ) followed by the preposition вАП„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО ( њal, вАЬagainstвАЭ) often refers to the action of building siegeworks against a city, that is, to besiege a city (e.g., Deut 20:2; 2 Kgs 25:1; Eccl 9:14; Jer 52:4; Ezek 4:2; 17:17; 21:27). It may occur with an explicit direct object, such as вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„Щ÷µ„ІвАО (dayeq, вАЬsiege wallвАЭ) or вАП„Ю÷Є„¶„Х÷є„®вАО (matsor, вАЬsiegeвАЭ), but here the direct object is implied.
n2500312: tn The verb вАП„†÷Є„І÷Ј„£вАО (naqaf, вАЬto surroundвАЭ) refers to the military action of surrounding a besieged city with army encampments to prevent anyone in it from escaping (2 Kgs 6:14; 11:8; Pss 17:9; 88:18; Job 19:6).
n2500313: tn Heb вАЬwith bitterness and hardship.вАЭ The nouns вАП„®÷є„Р„©„Б „Х÷Љ„™÷∞„Ь÷Є„Р÷Є„ФвАО (ro Њsh utela Њah, lit. вАЬbitterness and hardshipвАЭ) serve as adverbial accusatives of manner: вАЬwith bitterness and hardship.вАЭ These nouns вАП„®÷є„Р„©„Б „Х÷Љ„™÷∞„Ь÷Є„Р÷Є„ФвАО form a nominal hendiadys where the second retains its full nominal sense while the first functions adverbially: вАЬbitter hardship.вАЭ The noun II вАП„®÷є„Р„©„БвАО (ro Њsh, вАЬbitternessвАЭ) should not be confused with the common homonymic root I вАП„®÷є„Р„©„БвАО (ro Њsh, вАЬheadвАЭ). The noun вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷Є„Р÷Є„ФвАО (tela Њah, вАЬhardshipвАЭ) is used elsewhere in reference to the distress of Israel in Egypt (Num 20:14), in the wilderness (Exod 18:8), and in exile (Neh 9:32).
n2500314: tn The plural form of the noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ч÷≤„©„Б÷Ј„Ы÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (makhashakkim, вАЬdarknessesвАЭ) is an example of the plural of intensity (see IBHS 122 ¬І7.4.3a).
n2500315: tn The verb вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„У÷Ј„®вАО (gadar) has a twofold range of meaning: (1) вАЬto build up a wallвАЭ with stones, and (2) вАЬto block a roadвАЭ with a wall of stones. The imagery either depicts the Lord building a wall to seal off personified Jerusalem with no way to escape the city, or his blocking her road of escape. Siege imagery prevails in 3:4-6, but 3:7-9 pictures an unsuccessful escape that is thwarted due to blocked roads in 3:7 and 3:9.
n2500316: tn Heb вАЬhe has made heavy my chains.вАЭ
n2500317: tn Heb вАЬI call and I cry out.вАЭ The verbs вАП„Р÷ґ„Ц÷∞„Ґ÷Ј„І „Х÷Ј„Р÷≤„©„Б÷Ј„Х÷Љ÷µ„Ґ÷ЈвАО ( Њez њaq va Њashavvea њ, вАЬI call and I cry outвАЭ) form a verbal hendiadys where the second retains its full verbal sense while the first functions adverbially: вАЬI cry out desperately.вАЭ
n2500318: tn The verb вАП„©„Б„Х„ҐвАО (вАЬto cry outвАЭ) usually denotes calling out to God for help or deliverance from a lamentable plight (e.g., Job 30:20; 36:13; 38:41; Pss 5:3; 18:7, 42; 22:25; 28:2; 30:3; 31:23; 88:14; 119:147; Isa 58:9; Lam 3:8; Jon 2:3; Hab 1:2).
n2500319: tn The verb вАП„©„В÷Є„™÷Ј„ЭвАО (satam) is a hapax legomenon (term that appears in the Hebrew scriptures only once) meaning вАЬstop upвАЭ or вАЬshut out.вАЭ It functions as an idiom here, meaning вАЬhe has shut his ears to my prayerвАЭ (BDB 979 s.v.).
n2500320: tn The verb вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„У÷Ј„®вАО (gadar) has a twofold range of meanings: (1) вАЬto build up a wallвАЭ with stones, and (2) вАЬto block a roadвАЭ with a wall of stones. The collocated terms вАП„У÷Љ÷∞„®÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЩвАО (derakhay, вАЬmy roadsвАЭ) in 3:9 clearly indicate that the second category of meaning is in view.
n2500322: tn Heb вАЬhe has made my paths crooked.вАЭ The implication is that the paths by which one might escape cannot be traversed.
n2500323: tn Heb вАЬhe is to me [like] a bear lying in wait.вАЭ
n2500324: tc The Kethib is written вАП„Р÷Ј„®÷∞„Щ÷µ„ФвАО ( Њaryeh, вАЬlionвАЭ), while the Qere is вАП„Р÷≤„®÷і„ЩвАО ( Њari, вАЬlionвАЭ), simply a short spelling of the same term (BDB 71 s.v. вАП„Р÷Ј„®÷∞„Щ÷µ„ФвАО).
n2500326: tn Or вАЬhe made my paths deviate.вАЭ
n2500327: tn вАЬSince the Hebrew вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷∞„§÷Ј„©„Б÷Љ÷∞„Ч÷µ„†÷і„ЩвАО (vayefashekheni) occurs only here, and the translation relies on the Syriac and the Targum, it is not certain that the image of God as a predatory animal continues into this verse especially since [the beginning of the verse] is also of uncertain meaningвАЭ (D. R. Hillers, Lamentations [AB], 54).
n2500329: tn Heb вАЬand set me as the target.вАЭ
n2500330: tn The Hiphil stem of вАП„С„Х÷є„РвАО (bo Њ, lit., вАЬcause to come inвАЭ) here means вАЬto shootвАЭ arrows.
n2500331: tn Heb вАЬsons of his quiver.вАЭ This idiom refers to arrows (BDB 121 s.v. вАП„С÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО 6). The term вАЬsonвАЭ (вАП„С÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО, ben) is often used idiomatically with a following genitive, e.g., вАЬson of flameвАЭ = sparks (Job 5:7), вАЬson of a constellationвАЭ = stars (Job 38:22), вАЬson of a bowвАЭ = arrows (Job 41:2), вАЬson of a quiverвАЭ = arrows (Lam 3:13), and вАЬson of threshing-floorвАЭ = corn (Isa 21:10).
n2500332: tn Heb вАЬmy kidneys.вАЭ In Hebrew anthropology, the kidneys are often portrayed as the most sensitive and vital part of man. Poetic texts sometimes portray a person being fatally wounded by the Lord shooting arrows in his kidneys (Job 16:13; here in Lam 3:13). The equivalent English idiomatic counterpart is the heart, which is employed in the present translation.
n2500333: tc The MT reads вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО ( њammi, вАЬmy peopleвАЭ). Many medieval Hebrew mss read вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( њammim, вАЬpeoplesвАЭ), as reflected also in the Syriac Peshitta. The internal evidence (contextual congruence) favors the variant вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( њammim, вАЬpeoplesвАЭ).
n2500334: tn The noun вАП„†÷∞„Т÷і„Щ„†÷Є„ФвАО (neginah) is a musical term: (1) вАЬmusicвАЭ played on strings (Isa 38:20; Lam 5:14), (2) a technical musical term (Pss 4:1; 6:1; 54:1; 55:1; 67:1; 76:1; Hab 3:19) and (3) a вАЬmocking songвАЭ (Pss 69:13; 77:7; Job 30:9; Lam 3:14). The parallelism with вАП„©„В÷∞„Ч÷є„ІвАО (sekhoq, вАЬlaughingstockвАЭ) indicates that the latter category of meaning is in view.
n2500338: tn The Hiphil stem of вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„§÷Ј„©„БвАО (kafash) means вАЬto tread downвАЭ or вАЬmake someone cower.вАЭ It is rendered variously: вАЬtrampled me in the dustвАЭ (NIV), вАЬcovered me with ashesвАЭ (KJV, NKJV), вАЬground me into the dustвАЭ (NJPS), вАЬmade me cower in ashesвАЭ (RSV, NRSV), вАЬrubbed my face in the groundвАЭ (TEV), and вАЬrubbed me in the dirtвАЭ (CEV).
n2500339: tn Heb вАЬmy soul.вАЭ The term вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷і„ЩвАО (nafshi, вАЬmy soulвАЭ) is used as a synecdoche of part (= my soul) for the whole person (= I).
n2500340: tc The MT reads вАП„Х÷Ј„™÷Љ÷і„Ц÷∞„†÷Ј„ЧвАО (vattiznakh, вАЬshe/it rejectedвАЭ), resulting in the awkward phrase вАЬmy soul rejected from peace.вАЭ The LXX ќЇќ±бљґ бЉАѕАбљљѕГќ±ѕДќњ (kai ap≈Нsato) reflects a text of вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷і„Ц÷∞„†÷Ј„ЧвАО (vayyiznakh, вАЬhe deprived [my soul of peace].вАЭ The Latin Vulgate repulsa est reflects a text of вАП„Х÷Ј„™÷Љ÷і„Ц÷Љ÷Є„†÷Ј„ЧвАО (vattizzanakh), вАЬshe/it was excluded [from peace]вАЭ). Each is a form of вАП„Ц÷Є„†÷Ј„ЧвАО (zanakh, вАЬto rejectвАЭ). The MT and LXX read a Qal preterite but differ on whether the verb is feminine or masculine. The Vulgate read the same consonants as in the MT but as a Niphal, and so passive. The MT best explains the origin of the LXX and Vulgate readings. The вАП„ЮвАО (mem) beginning the next word may have been an enclitic on the verb rather than a preposition on the noun. This would be the only Qal occurrence of вАП„Ц÷Є„†÷Ј„ЧвАО (zanakh) used with the preposition вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min). Placing the вАП„ЮвАО (mem) on the noun would have created the confusion leading to the changes made by the LXX and Vulgate. HALOT 276 s.v. II вАП„Ц„†„ЧвАО attempts to deal with the problem lexically by positing a meaning вАЬto exclude fromвАЭ for вАП„Ц÷Є„†÷Ј„ЧвАО (zanakh) plus вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min), but also allows that the Niphal may be the correct reading.
n2500341: tn Heb вАЬfrom peace.вАЭ H. Hummel suggests that вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь„Х÷є„ЭвАО (shalom) is the object and the вАП„ЮвАО (mem) is not the preposition вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min), but an enclitic on the verb (вАЬEnclitic Mem in Early Northwest Semitic, Especially in HebrewвАЭ JBL 76 [1957]: 105). вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь„Х÷є„ЭвАО (shalom) has a wide range of meaning. The connotation is that there is no peace within; the speaker is too troubled for any calm to take hold.
n2500343: tn Heb вАЬand my hope from the Lord.вАЭ The hope is for deliverance. The words вАЬI have lost allвАЭ have been supplied in the translation in order to clarify the Hebrew idiom for the English reader.
n2500344:
tc The LXX records бЉРќЉќљбљµѕГќЄќЈќљ (emnƒУsthƒУn, вАЬI rememberedвАЭ), which may reflect a first person singular form вАП„Ц÷Є„Ы÷Ј„®÷∞„™÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (zakharti), whereas the MT preserves the form вАП„Ц÷∞„Ы÷Є„®вАО (zekhor), which may be Qal imperative second person masculine singular (вАЬRemember!вАЭ) or infinitive construct (вАЬTo rememberвА¶вАЭ). A second person masculine singular imperative would most likely address God. In the next verse вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷і„ЩвАО (nafshi, вАЬmy soulвАЭ) is the subject of вАП„Ц÷∞„Ы÷Є„®вАО (zekhor). If вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷і„ЩвАО (nafshi) is also the subject here, one would expect a second person feminine singular imperative вАП„Ц÷і„Ы÷∞„®÷і„ЩвАО (zikhri), a form that stands in the middle of the MTвАЩs вАП„Ц÷∞„Ы÷Є„®вАО (zekhor) and the presumed вАП„Ц÷Є„Ы÷Ј„®÷∞„™÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (zakharti) read by the LXX. English versions are split between the options: вАЬTo recallвАЭ (NJPS), вАЬRemember!вАЭ (RSV, NRSV, NASB), вАЬRememberingвАЭ (KJV, NKJV), and вАЬI rememberвАЭ (NIV).
tn The basic meaning of вАП„Ц÷Є„Ы÷Ј„®вАО (zakhar) is вАЬto remember, call to mindвАЭ (HALOT 270 s.v. I вАП„Ц„Ы„®вАО). Although it is often used in reference to recollection of past events, it can also describe consideration of present situations: вАЬto consider, think aboutвАЭ something present (BDB 270 s.v. 5).
n2500345: tn The two nouns вАП„Ґ÷Є„†÷∞„Щ÷і„Щ „Х÷Љ„Ю÷∞„®„Х÷Љ„У÷і„ЩвАО ( њonyi umerudi, lit., вАЬmy poverty and my homelessnessвАЭ) form a nominal hendiadys in which one noun functions adjectivally and the other retains its full nominal sense: вАЬmy impoverished homelessnessвАЭ or вАЬhomeless poorвАЭ (GKC 397-98 ¬І124.e). The use of a nearly identical phrase in Lam 1:7 and Isa 58:7 (see GKC 226 ¬І83.c) suggests this was a Hebrew idiom. JerusalemвАЩs inhabitants were impoverished and homeless.
n2500346: tn The two nouns joined by вАП„ХвАО (vav), вАП„Ь÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„†÷Є„Ф „Х÷Є„®÷є„Р„©„БвАО (la њana varo Њsh, вАЬwormwood and poisonвАЭ) form a nominal hendiadys. The first retains its full verbal sense and the second functions adjectivally: вАЬbitter poison.вАЭ
n2500347:
tc The MT reads вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷і„ЩвАО (nafshi, вАЬmy soulвАЭ); however, the Masoretic scribes preserve an alternate textual tradition, marked by the Tiqqune Sopherim (вАЬcorrections by the scribesвАЭ), of вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷ґ„Ъ÷ЄвАО (nafshekha, вАЬyour soulвАЭ).
tn Heb вАЬmy soul.вАЭ The term вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷і„ЩвАО (nafshi, вАЬmy soulвАЭ) is used as a synecdoche of part (= my soul) for the whole person (= I). The verb вАП„™÷Љ÷і„Ц÷∞„Ы÷Љ„Х÷є„®вАО (tizkor) is Qal imperfect third person feminine singular, and the subject is вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷і„ЩвАО (nafshi, вАЬmy soulвАЭ), though the term does not appear until the end of the verse, functioning as the subject of both verbs. Due to the synecdoche, the line is translated as though the verb were first person common singular.
n2500348: tn The infinitive absolute followed by an imperfect of the same root is an emphatic rhetorical statement: вАП„Ц÷Є„Ы„Х÷є„® „™÷Љ÷і„Ц÷∞„Ы÷Љ„Х÷є„®вАО (zakhor tizkor, вАЬcontinually thinkвАЭ). Although the basic meaning of вАП„Ц÷Є„Ы÷Ј„®вАО (zakhar) is вАЬto remember, call to mindвАЭ (HALOT 270 I вАП„Ц„Ы„®вАО), here it refers to consideration of a present situation: вАЬto consider, think aboutвАЭ something present (BDB 270 s.v. вАП„Ц÷Є„Ы÷Ј„®вАО 5). The referent of the third person feminine singular form of вАП„™÷Љ÷і„Ц÷∞„Ы÷Љ„Х÷є„®вАО (tizkor) is the feminine singular noun вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷і„ЩвАО (nafshi, вАЬmy soulвАЭ).
n2500349:
tc The MT reads вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷і„ЩвАО (nafshi, вАЬmy soulвАЭ); however, the Masoretic scribes preserve an alternate textual tradition, included in some lists of the Tiqqune Sopherim (вАЬcorrections by the scribesвАЭ), of вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷ґ„Ъ÷ЄвАО (nafshekha, вАЬyour soulвАЭ).
tn Heb вАЬmy soulвА¶вАЭ or вАЬyour soulвА¶вАЭ The term вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷і„ЩвАО (nafshi, вАЬmy soulвАЭ) is used as a synecdoche of part (= my soul) for the whole person (= I). Likewise, вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷ґ„Ъ÷ЄвАО (nafshekha, вАЬyour soulвАЭ) is also a synecdoche of part (= your soul) for the whole person (= you).
n2500350:
tc The MT preserves the Kethib вАП„Х÷∞„™÷Є„©„Б÷і„Щ„Ч÷ЈвАО (vetashiakh), while the Qere reads вАП„Х÷∞„™÷Є„©„Б„Х÷є„Ч÷ЈвАО (vetashoakh). In theory the Kethib could be a Qal or Hiphil of a root вАП„©„Б÷і„Щ„ЧвАО (shikh, вАЬmelt away, despairвАЭ) or the Hiphil of a root вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„ЧвАО (shukh, вАЬto sink down, collapseвАЭ), while the Qere is a Qal of a root вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„ЧвАО (shukh) or of вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ч÷Ј„ЧвАО (shakhakh, вАЬstoop down, be bent overвАЭ). None of these are common roots, and one or more may be byforms of each other. The conjectured meaning for вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (shuakh) in BDB 1005 s.v вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО is that of вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ч÷Ј„ЧвАО (shakhakh). HALOT 1438-39 s.v. вАП„©„Б„Х„ЧвАО reads the root as вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ч÷Ј„ЧвАО (shakhakh). The various options yield similar meanings.
tn Heb вАЬand my soul sinks down within me.вАЭ The verb II вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (shuakh, вАЬto sink downвАЭ) is used here in a figurative sense, meaning вАЬto be depressed.вАЭ
n2500352: tn Heb вАЬto my heart.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ь÷µ„С÷Є„СвАО (levav, вАЬheartвАЭ) has a broad range of meanings, including its use as a metonymy of association, standing for thoughts and thinking (= вАЬmindвАЭ) (e.g., Deut 32:46; 1 Chr 29:18; Job 17:11; Ps 73:7; Isa 10:7; Hag 1:5, 7; 2:15, 18; Zech 7:10; 8:17).
n2500353: tn It is difficult to capture the nuances of the Hebrew word вАП„Ч÷ґ„°÷ґ„УвАО (khesed). When used of the Lord it is often connected to his covenant loyalty. This is the only occasion when the plural form of вАП„Ч÷ґ„°÷ґ„УвАО (khesed) precedes the plural form of вАП„®÷Ј„Ч÷≤„Ю÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (rakhamim, вАЬmercy, compassionвАЭ). The plural forms, as with this one, tend to be in late texts. The plural may indicate several concrete expressions of GodвАЩs kindnesses or may indicate the abstract concept of his kindness.
n2500354: tc The MT reads вАП„™÷Є„Ю÷∞„†„Х÷ЉвАО (tamenu) as, вАЬwe are [not] cut off,вАЭ Qal perfect first person common plural from вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЭвАО (tamam, вАЬbe finishedвАЭ): вАЬ[Because of] the kindnesses of the Lord, indeed we are not cut off.вАЭ However, the ancient versions (LXX, Syriac Peshitta, Aramaic Targum) and many medieval Hebrew mss preserve the alternate reading вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷Љ„Х÷ЉвАО (tammu), a third person common plural form of the same root and stem: вАЬThe kindnesses of the Lord indeed never cease.вАЭ The external evidence favors the alternate reading. The internal evidence supports this as well, as the parallel B-line suggests, вАЬhis compassions never come to an end.вАЭ Several English versions follow the MT: вАЬIt is of the LordвАЩs mercies that we are not consumedвАЭ (KJV, NKJV), вАЬBecause of the LordвАЩs great love we are not consumedвАЭ (NIV). Other English versions follow the alternate textual tradition: вАЬThe steadfast love of the Lord never ceasesвАЭ (RSV, NRSV), вАЬThe LordвАЩs lovingkindnesses indeed never ceaseвАЭ (NASB), вАЬThe kindness of the Lord has not endedвАЭ (NJPS) and вАЬThe LordвАЩs unfailing love still continuesвАЭ (TEV).
n2500355: tn The plural form of вАП„®÷Ј„Ч÷≤„Ю÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (rakhamim) may denote the abstract concept of mercy, several concrete expressions of mercy, or the plural of intensity: вАЬgreat compassion.вАЭ See IBHS 122 ¬І7.4.3a.
n2500357: tn The adjective вАП„®÷Ј„СвАО (rav) has a broad range of meanings: (1) quantitative: вАЬmuch, numerous, many (with plurals), abundant, enough, exceedinglyвАЭ and (2) less often in a qualitative sense: вАЬgreatвАЭ (a) of space and location, (b) вАЬstrongвАЭ as opposed to вАЬweakвАЭ and (c) вАЬmajor.вАЭ The traditional translation, вАЬgreat is thy faithfulness,вАЭ should be understood in a quantitative sense: вАЬyour faithfulness is abundantвАЭ [or, вАЬplentifulвАЭ]. NJPS correctly translates, вАЬAmple is your grace!вАЭ
n2500358: tn Heb вАЬMy soul saidвА¶вАЭ The term вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷і„ЩвАО (nafshi, вАЬmy soulвАЭ) is a synecdoche of a part (= my soul) for the whole person (= I).
n2500360: tn Heb вАЬto the soulвА¶вАЭ The term вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh, вАЬsoulвАЭ) is a synecdoche of a part (= вАЬthe soul who seeks himвАЭ) for the whole person (= вАЬthe person who seeks himвАЭ).
n2500361: tn Heb вАЬwaiting and silently.вАЭ The two main words (plus two conjunctions) вАП„Х÷∞„Щ÷Є„Ч÷і„Щ„Ь „Х÷∞„У„Х÷Љ„Ю÷Є„ЭвАО (veyakhil vedumam, вАЬwaiting and silentlyвАЭ) form a hendiadys where the first functions verbally and the second adverbially: вАЬto wait silently.вАЭ The adverb вАП„У„Х÷Љ„Ю÷Є„ЭвАО (dumam, вАЬsilentlyвАЭ) also functions as a metonymy of association, standing for patience or rest (HALOT 217 s.v.). This metonymical nuance is captured well in less literal English versions: вАЬwait in patienceвАЭ (TEV) and вАЬwait patientlyвАЭ (CEV, NJPS). The more literal English versions do not express the metonymy as well: вАЬquietly waitвАЭ (KJV, NKJV, ASV), вАЬwaits silentlyвАЭ (NASB), and вАЬwait quietlyвАЭ (RSV, NRSV, NIV).
n2500362: tn Heb вАЬdeliverance of the Lord.вАЭ In the genitive-construct, the genitive вАП„Щ„Ф„Х„ФвАО (YHWH, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) denotes source; that is, he is the source of the deliverance: вАЬdeliverance from the Lord.вАЭ
n2500363: tn See note at 3:1 on the Hebrew term for вАЬmanвАЭ here.
n2500365: sn Jeremiah is referring to the painful humiliation of subjugation to the Babylonians, particularly to the exile of the populace of Jerusalem. The Babylonians and Assyrians frequently used the phrase вАЬbear the yokeвАЭ as a metaphor: their subjects were made as subservient to them as yoked oxen were to their masters. Because the Babylonian exile would last for seventy years, only those who were in their youth when Jerusalem fell would have any hope of living until the return of the remnant. For the middle-aged and elderly, the yoke of exile would be insufferable, but those who bore this вАЬyokeвАЭ in their youth would have hope.
n2500366: tn Heb вАЬin his youth.вАЭ The preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) functions in a temporal sense: вАЬwhen.вАЭ
n2500367: tn Heb вАЬhim.вАЭ The speaking voice in this chapter continues to be that of the вАП„Т÷Љ÷ґ„С÷ґ„®вАО (gever, вАЬmanвАЭ). The image of female Jerusalem in chs. 1-2 was fluid, being able to refer to the city or its inhabitants, both female and male. So too the вАЬdefeated soldierвАЭ or вАЬeverymanвАЭ (see note at 3:1 on вАЬmanвАЭ) is fluid and can represent any member of the Jewish community, male and female. This line especially has a proverbial character that can be extended to any person, hence the translation. But masculine pronouns are otherwise maintained, reflecting the Hebrew grammatical system and the speaking voice of the poem.
n2500368: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (the Lord) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2500369: tn Heb вАЬhas laid it on him.вАЭ The verb вАП„†÷Є„Ш÷Ј„ЬвАО (natal) is used four times in Biblical Hebrew; the related noun refers to heaviness or a burden. The entry of BDB 642 s.v. is outdated, while HALOT 694 s.v. вАП„†„Ш„ЬвАО is acceptable for the Qal. See D. R. Hillers, Lamentations (AB), 57. HillersвАЩ suggestion of a stative meaning for the Qal is followed here, although вАЬimposeвАЭ is also possible based on 2 Sam 24:12.
n2500370: tn Heb вАЬLet him put his mouth in the dust.вАЭ
n2500372: tc The MT reads вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) here rather than вАП„Щ„Ф„Х„ФвАО (YHWH, вАЬthe LordвАЭ). See the tc note at 1:14.
n2500373: tn The verse is unusually short, and something unrecoverable may be missing.
n2500374: tn Heb вАЬAlthough he has caused grief.вАЭ The word вАЬusвАЭ is added in the translation.
n2500375: tn Heb вАЬHe will have compassion.вАЭ The words вАЬon usвАЭ are added in the translation.
n2500376: tc The Kethib preserves the singular form вАП„Ч÷Ј„°÷∞„У÷Љ„Х÷євАО (khasdo, вАЬhis kindnessвАЭ), also reflected in the LXX and Aramaic Targum. The Qere reads the plural form вАП„Ч÷≤„°÷Є„У÷Є„Щ„ХвАО (khasadayv, вАЬhis kindnessesвАЭ), which is reflected in the Latin Vulgate.
n2500377: tn Heb вАЬhe does not afflict from his heart.вАЭ The term вАП„Ь÷µ„С÷Є„СвАО (levav, вАЬheartвАЭ) preceded by the preposition вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min) most often describes oneвАЩs initiative or motivation, e.g. вАЬof oneвАЩs own accordвАЭ (Num 16:28; 24:13; Deut 4:9; 1 Kings 12:33; Neh 6:8; Job 8:10; Isa 59:13; Ezek 13:2, 17). It is not GodвАЩs internal motivation to bring calamity and trouble upon people.
n2500379: tn Heb вАЬprisoners of earth/land.вАЭ The term вАП„Р÷ґ„®÷ґ„•вАО ( Њerets) may refer to (1) the earth, (2) a country, or (3) the promised land in particular (as well as other referents). вАЬEarthвАЭ is chosen here since the context presents GodвАЩs general principles in dealing with humanity. Given the historical circumstances, however, prisoners from the land of Israel are certainly in the background.
n2500380: tn The speaking voice is still that of the вАП„Т÷Љ÷ґ„С÷ґ„®вАО (gever, вАЬmanвАЭ), but the context and line are more universal in character.
n2500381: tn Heb вАЬto turn away a manвАЩs justice,вАЭ that is, the justice or equitable judgment he would receive. See the previous note regarding the вАЬman.вАЭ
n2500382: tc The MT reads вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) here rather than вАП„Щ„Ф„Х„ФвАО (YHWH, вАЬthe LordвАЭ). See the tc note at 1:14.
n2500383: tn Heb вАЬthe Lord does not see.вАЭ The verb вАП„®÷Є„Р÷Є„ФвАО (ra Њah, вАЬto seeвАЭ) is here used in reference to mental observation and approval: вАЬto gaze atвАЭ with joy and pleasure (e.g., 2 Kgs 10:16; Mic 7:9; Jer 29:32; Isa 52:8; Job 20:17; 33:28; Pss 54:9 HT [54:7 ET]; 106:5; 128:5; Song 3:11; 6:11; Eccl 2:1). If the line is parallel to the end of v. 35, then a circumstantial clause, вАЬthe Lord not seeing,вАЭ would be appropriate. The infinitives in 34-36 would then depend on the verbs in v. 33; see D. R. Hillers, Lamentations (AB), 71.
n2500384: tn Heb вАЬWho is this, he spoke and it came to pass?вАЭ The general sense is to ask whose commands are fulfilled. The phrase вАЬhe spoke and it came to passвАЭ is taken as an allusion to the creation account (see Gen 1:3).
n2500385: tc The MT reads вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) here rather than вАП„Щ„Ф„Х„ФвАО (YHWH, вАЬthe LordвАЭ). See the tc note at 1:14.
n2500386: tn Heb вАЬFrom the mouth of the Most High does it not go forth, both evil and good?вАЭ
n2500387: tn The Hebrew word here is вАП„Р÷Є„У÷Є„ЭвАО ( Њadam), which can mean вАЬmanвАЭ or вАЬperson.вАЭ The second half of the line is more personalized to the speaking voice of the defeated soldier by using вАП„Т÷Љ÷ґ„С÷ґ„®вАО (gever, вАЬmanвАЭ). See the note at 3:1.
n2500388:
tc The Kethib has the singular вАП„Ч÷ґ„Ш÷∞„Р„Х÷євАО (khet Њo, вАЬhis sinвАЭ), which is reflected in the LXX. The Qere reads the plural вАП„Ч÷≤„Ш÷Є„Р÷Є„Щ„ХвАО (khata Њayv, вАЬhis sinsвАЭ), which is preserved in many medieval Hebrew mss and reflected in the other early versions (Aramaic Targum, Syriac Peshitta, Latin Vulgate). The external and internal evidence are not decisive in favor of either reading.
tn Heb вАЬconcerning his punishment.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ч÷µ„Ш÷∞„РвАО (khet Њ) has a broad range of meanings: (1) вАЬsin,вАЭ (2) вАЬguilt of sinвАЭ and (3) вАЬpunishment for sin,вАЭ which fits the context of calamity as discipline and punishment for sin (e.g., Lev 19:17; 20:20; 22:9; 24:15; Num 9:13; 18:22, 32; Isa 53:12; Ezek 23:49). The metonymical (cause-effect) relation between sin and punishment is clear in the expressions вАП„Ч÷µ„Ш÷∞„Р „Ю÷і„©„Б÷∞„§÷Ј„Ш÷Њ„Ю÷Є„Х÷ґ„™вАО (khet Њ mishpat mavet, вАЬsin deserving death penalty,вАЭ Deut 21:22) and вАП„Ч÷µ„Ш÷∞„Р „Ю÷Є„Х÷ґ„™вАО (khet Њ mavet, вАЬsin unto death,вАЭ Deut 22:26). The point of this verse is that the punishment of sin can sometimes lead to death; therefore, anyone who is being punished by God for his sins, and yet lives, has little to complain about.
n2500389: tn Heb вАЬLet us test our ways and examine.вАЭ The two verbs вАП„Х÷∞„†÷Ј„Ч÷∞„І÷є„®÷Є„ФвАОвА¶вАП„†÷Ј„Ч÷∞„§÷Љ÷∞„©„В÷Є„ФвАО (nakhpesahвА¶venakhqorah, вАЬLet us test and let us examineвАЭ) form a verbal hendiadys in which the first functions adverbially and the second retains its full verbal force: вАЬLet us carefully examine our ways.вАЭ
n2500390: tc The MT reads the singular noun вАП„Ь÷∞„С÷Є„С÷µ„†„Х÷ЉвАО (levavenu, вАЬour heartвАЭ), but the ancient versions (LXX, Aramaic Targum, Latin Vulgate) and many medieval Hebrew mss read the plural noun вАП„Ь÷∞„С÷Є„С÷µ„Щ„†„Х÷ЉвАО (levavenu, вАЬour heartsвАЭ). Hebrew regularly places plural pronouns on singular nouns used as collectives (135 times on the singular вАЬheartвАЭ and only twice on the plural вАЬheartsвАЭ). The plural вАЬheartsвАЭ is actually rather rare in any Hebrew construction. The LXX renders similar Hebrew constructions (singular вАЬheartвАЭ plus a plural pronoun) with the plural вАЬheartsвАЭ about 1/3 of the time; therefore it cannot be considered evidence for the reading. The Vulgate may have been influenced by the LXX. Although a distributive sense is appropriate for a much higher percentage of passages using the plural вАЬheartsвАЭ in the LXX, no clear reason for the differentiation in the LXX has emerged. Likely the singular Hebrew form is original, but the meaning is best represented in English with the plural.
n2500391: tn The Heb emphasizes the pronoun вАЬWeвАФwe have sinnedвА¶.вАЭ Given the contrast with the following, it means, вАЬFor our part, we have sinnedвА¶.вАЭ A poetic reading in English would place vocal emphasis on вАЬweвАЭ followed by a short pause.
n2500392: tn Heb вАЬWe have revolted and we have rebelled.вАЭ The two verbs вАП„§÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„Ґ÷∞„†„Х÷Љ „Х÷Љ„Ю÷Є„®÷і„Щ„†„Х÷ЉвАО (pasha њnu umarinu, вАЬwe have revolted and we have rebelledвАЭ) form a verbal hendiadys in which the synonyms emphasize the single idea.
n2500393: tn The Hebrew emphasizes the pronoun: вАЬYouвАФyou have not forgiven.вАЭ Given the contrast with the preceding, it means, вАЬFor your part, you have not forgiven.вАЭ A poetic reading in English would place vocal emphasis on вАЬyouвАЭ followed by a short pause.
n2500394: tn Heb вАЬcovered.вАЭ The object must be supplied either from the next line (вАЬcovered yourselfвАЭ) or from the end of this line (вАЬcovered usвАЭ).
n2500395: tn Heb вАЬoffscouring and refuse.вАЭ The two nouns вАП„°÷∞„Ч÷і„Щ „Х÷Љ„Ю÷Є„Р„Х÷є„°вАО (sekhi uma Њos) probably form a nominal hendiadys in which the first noun functions as an adjective and the second retains its full nominal sense: вАЬfilthy refuse,вАЭ i.e., вАЬfilthy scum.вАЭ
n2500398: tn The similar sounding nouns вАП„§÷Љ÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„У „Х÷Є„§÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„™вАО (pakhad vafakhat, вАЬpanic and pitfallвАЭ) are an example of paronomasia.
n2500399: tn Similar to the paronomasia in the preceding line, the words вАП„Ф÷Ј„©„Б÷Љ÷µ„Р„™ „Х÷∞„Ф÷Ј„©„Б÷Љ÷Є„С÷ґ„®вАО (hashe Њt vehashaver, вАЬdevastation and destructionвАЭ) form an example of alliteration: the beginning of the words sound alike.
n25003100: tn Heb вАЬcanals.вАЭ The phrase вАЬcanals of waterвАЭ (eye water = tears) is an example of hyperbole. The English idiom вАЬstreams of tearsвАЭ is also hyperbolic.
n25003101: tn Heb вАЬmy eyes flow down with canals of water.вАЭ
n25003102: tn Heb вАЬthe daughter of my people,вАЭ or вАЬthe Daughter, my people.вАЭ
n25003103: tn Heb вАЬbecause of the destruction of [the daughter of my people].вАЭ
n25003104: tn Heb вАЬmy eye flows.вАЭ The term вАЬeyeвАЭ is a metonymy of association, standing for the вАЬtearsвАЭ which flow from oneвАЩs eyes.
n25003105: tn Heb вАЬwithout stopping.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ф÷Ј„§„Х÷Љ„Т÷Є„ФвАО (hafugah, вАЬstopвАЭ) is a hapax legomenon (word that occurs only once in Hebrew scriptures). The form of the noun is unusual, probably being derived from the denominative Hiphil verbal stem of the root вАП„§÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ТвАО (pug, вАЬto grow weary, ineffective, numb; become coldвАЭ).
n25003106: tn The phrase вАЬwhat has happenedвАЭ is added in the translation for smoother English style and readability.
n25003107: tn Heb вАЬmy eye causes grief to my soul.вАЭ The term вАЬeyeвАЭ is a metonymy of association, standing for that which one sees with the eyes.
n25003108: tn Heb вАЬmy soul.вАЭ The term вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷і„ЩвАО (nafshi, вАЬmy soulвАЭ) is a synecdoche of a part (= my soul) for the whole person (= me).
n25003109: tn Heb вАЬat the sight of all the daughters of my city.вАЭ It is understood that seeing the plight of the women, not simply seeing the women, is what is so grievous. To make this clear, вАЬsufferingвАЭ was supplied in the translation.
n25003111: tn The construction вАП„¶„Х÷є„У „¶÷Є„У„Х÷Љ„†÷і„ЩвАО (tsod tsaduni, вАЬthey have hunted me downвАЭ) with the paronomastic infinitive absolute is emphatic.
n25003113: tn Heb вАЬI said,вАЭ meaning вАЬI said to myselfвАЭ = вАЬI thought.вАЭ
n25003114: tn Heb вАЬI was about to be cut off.вАЭ The verb вАП„†÷і„Т÷∞„Ц÷Є„®÷∞„™÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (nigzarti), Niphal perfect first person common singular from вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„Ц÷Ј„®вАО (gazar, вАЬto be cut offвАЭ), functions in an ingressive sense: вАЬabout to be cut off.вАЭ It is used in reference to the threat of death (e.g., Ezek 37:11; Ps 88:5). To be вАЬcut offвАЭ from the land of the living means to experience death (Isa 53:8).
n25003115: tn Heb вАЬfrom a pit of lowest places.вАЭ
n25003116: tn The verb could be understood as a precative (вАЬHear my pleaвАЭ) parallel to the following volitive verb, вАЬdo not close.вАЭ
n25003118: tn The preposition вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) continues syntactically from вАЬmy pleaвАЭ in the previous line (e.g. Ex 5:2; Josh 22:2; 1 Sam 8:7; 12:1; Jer 43:4).
n25003119: tn The verb could be understood as a precative (вАЬDraw nearвАЭ). The perspective of the poem seems to be that of prayer during distress rather than a testimony that God has delivered someone.
n25003120: tn The verb could be understood as a precative (вАЬSayвАЭ).
n25003121: tc The MT reads вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) here rather than вАП„Щ„Ф„Х„ФвАО (YHWH, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) as in the following verse. See the tc note at 1:14.
n25003122: tn This verb, like others in this stanza, could be understood as a precative (вАЬPleadвАЭ).
n25003123: tn Heb вАЬthe causes of my soul.вАЭ The term вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷і„ЩвАО (nafshi, вАЬmy soulвАЭ) is a synecdoche of a part (= my soul) for the whole person (= me).
n25003125: tc The MT reads вАП„Ь÷і„ЩвАО (li, вАЬto meвАЭ), but many medieval Hebrew mss and the ancient versions (Aramaic Targum, Syriac Peshitta, Latin Vulgate) all reflect a Vorlage of вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ь÷Є„ЩвАО ( њalay, вАЬagainst meвАЭ).
n25003126: tn The verb could be understood as a precative (вАЬHearвАЭ).
n25003127: tn Heb вАЬthe lips of my assailants and their thoughts.вАЭ
n25003128: tn Heb вАЬtheir rising and their sitting.вАЭ The two terms вАП„©„Б÷і„С÷∞„™÷Љ÷Є„Э „Х÷∞„І÷і„Щ„Ю÷Є„™÷Є„ЭвАО (shivtam veqimatam, вАЬtheir sitting and their risingвАЭ) form a merism: two terms that are polar opposites are used to encompass everything in between. The idiom вАЬfrom your rising to your sittingвАЭ refers to the earliest action in the morning and the latest action in the evening (e.g., Deut 6:7; Ps 139:3). The enemies mock Jerusalem from the moment they arise in the morning until the moment they sit down in the evening.
n25003129: tn Heb вАЬPlease cause to return.вАЭ The imperfect verb вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„©„Б÷і„Щ„СвАО (tashiv), Hiphil imperfect second person masculine singular from вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„СвАО (shuv, вАЬto returnвАЭ), functions in a volitional sense, like an imperative of request. The Hiphil stem of вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„СвАО (shuv, in the Hiphil вАЬto cause to returnвАЭ) often means вАЬto make requital, to pay backвАЭ (e.g., Judg 9:5, 56; 1 Sam 25:39; 1 Kgs 2:32, 44; Neh 3:36 HT [4:4 ET]; Prov 24:12, 29; Hos 12:3; Joel 4:4, 7 HT [3:4, 7 ET]) (BDB 999 s.v. вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„СвАО 4.a).
n25003130: tn Heb вАЬrecompense to them.вАЭ The noun вАП„Т÷Љ÷∞„Ю„Х÷Љ„ЬвАО (gemul, вАЬdealing, accomplishmentвАЭ) has two metonymical (cause-effect) meanings: (1) positive вАЬbenefitвАЭ and (2) negative вАЬretribution, requital, recompense,вАЭ the sense used here (e.g., Pss 28:4; 94:2; 137:8; Prov 19:17; Isa 35:4; 59:18; 66:6; Jer 51:6; Lam 3:64; Joel 4:4, 7 HT [3:4, 7 ET]). The phrase вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„©„Б÷і„Щ„С „Т÷Љ÷∞„Ю„Х÷Љ„ЬвАО (tashiv gemul) means вАЬto pay back retributionвАЭ (e.g., Joel 4:4, 7 HT [3:4, 7 ET]), that is, to return the deeds of the wicked upon them as a display of talionic or poetic justice.
n25003131: tn Heb вАЬtheir hands.вАЭ The term вАЬhandвАЭ is a synecdoche of a part (= hands) for the whole person (= they).
n25003132: tn Heb вАЬaccording to the work of their hands.вАЭ
n25003133: tn The noun вАП„Ю÷∞„Т÷і„†÷Љ÷Ј„ФвАО (meginnah) is a hapax legomenon. Its meaning is debated; earlier lexicographers suggested that it meant вАЬcoveringвАЭ (BDB 171 s.v.), but more recent lexicons suggest вАЬshamelessnessвАЭ or вАЬinsanityвАЭ (HALOT 546 s.v.). The translation is based on the term being parallel to вАЬcurseвАЭ and needing to relate to вАЬheart.вАЭ Cf. NRSVвАЩs вАЬanguish of heart.вАЭ
n25003134: tn Heb вАЬpursue.вАЭ The accusative direct object is implied in the Hebrew and inserted in the translation.
n250041: sn According to W. F. Lanahan (вАЬThe Speaking Voice in the Book of LamentationsвАЭ JBL 93 [1974]: 48), the persona or speaking voice in chap. 4 is a bourgeois, the common man. This voice is somewhat akin to the reporter in chs 1-2 in that much of the description is in the third person. However, вАЬthe bourgeois has some sense of identity with his fellow-citizens,вАЭ seen in the shift to the first person plural. The alphabetic acrostic structure reduces to two bicola per letter. The first letter of only the first line in each stanza spells the acrostic.
n250043: tn Heb вАЬhad grown dim.вАЭ The verb вАП„Щ„Х÷Љ„Ґ÷Ј„ЭвАО (yuвАЩam), Hophal imperfect third person masculine singular from вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЭвАО (вАЩamam, вАЬto conceal, darkenвАЭ), literally means вАЬto be dimmedвАЭ or вАЬto be darkened.вАЭ Most English versions render this literally: the gold has вАЬbecome dimвАЭ (KJV, NKJV), вАЬgrown dimвАЭ (RSV, NRSV), вАЬis dulledвАЭ (NJPS), and вАЬgrown dullвАЭ (TEV); however, the NIV has captured the sense well: вАЬHow the gold has lost its luster.вАЭ
n250044:
tc The verb вАП„Щ÷і„©„Б÷∞„†÷ґ„РвАО (yishne Њ, Qal imperfect third person feminine singular) is typically taken to be the only Qal imperfect of I вАП„©„Б÷Є„†÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО (shanah). Such a spelling with вАП„РвАО (alef) instead of вАП„ФвАО (he) is feasible. D. R. Hillers suggests the root вАП„©„В÷Є„†÷µ„РвАО (sane Њ, вАЬto hateвАЭ): вАЬPure gold is hated.вАЭ This maintains the consonantal text and also makes sense in context. In either case the point is that gold no longer holds the same value, probably because there is nothing available to buy with it.
tn Heb вАЬchanges.вАЭ The imagery in this verse about gold is without parallel in the Bible and uncertain in precise nuance.
n250045: tn Heb вАЬthe stones of holiness/jewelry.вАЭ вАП„І÷є„У÷ґ„©„БвАО (qodesh) in most cases refers to holiness or sacredness. For the meaning вАЬjewelryвАЭ see J. A. Emerton, вАЬThe Meaning of вАП„Р÷Ј„С÷∞„†÷µ„Щ÷Њ„І÷є„У÷ґ„©„БвАО in Lamentations 4:1вАЭ ZAW 79 (1967): 233-36.
n250046: tn Heb вАЬat the head of every street.вАЭ
n250048: tn Heb вАЬthe work of the hands of a potter.вАЭ
n250049: tn The noun вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„†÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (tannin) means вАЬjackals.вАЭ The plural ending вАП÷Њ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (-in) is diminutive (GKC 242 ¬І87.e) (e.g., Lam 1:4).
n2500410: tn Heb вАЬdraw out the breast and suckle their young.вАЭ
n2500412: tc The MT Kethib form вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„Щ „Ґ÷µ„†÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (ki  њenim) is by all accounts a variation from an original text of вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Ј„Щ÷∞„Ґ÷µ„†÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (kay њenim, вАЬlike ostrichesвАЭ) which is preserved in the Qere and the medieval Hebrew mss, and reflected in the LXX.
n2500413: tn Heb вАЬbread.вАЭ The term вАЬbreadвАЭ might function as a synecdoche of specific (= bread) for general (= food); however, the following parallel line does indeed focus on the act of breaking bread in two.
n2500414: tn Heb вАЬthere is not a divider to them.вАЭ The term вАЬdividerвАЭ refers to the action of breaking bread in two before giving it to a person to eat (Isa 58:7; Jer 16:7; Lam 4:4).
n2500415: tn Heb вАЬeaters of delicacies.вАЭ An alternate English gloss would be вАЬconnoisseurs of fine foods.вАЭ
n2500418: tn Heb вАЬin purple.вАЭ The term вАП„™„Х÷є„Ь÷Є„ҐвАО (tola њ, вАЬpurpleвАЭ) is a figurative description of expensive clothing, a metonymy of association where the color of the dyed clothes (= purple) stands for the clothes themselves.
n2500419: tn Heb вАЬembrace garbage.вАЭ One may also translate вАЬrummage throughвАЭ (cf. NCV вАЬpick through trash pilesвАЭ; TEV вАЬpawing through refuseвАЭ; NLT вАЬsearch the garbage pitsвАЭ).
n2500420: tn The Hebrew word вАП„Р÷Ј„©„Б÷∞„§÷Љ÷Ј„™÷Љ„Х÷є„™вАО ( Њashpatot) can also mean вАЬash heaps.вАЭ Though not used as a combination elsewhere, to вАЬembrace ash heapsвАЭ might also envision a state of mourning or even dead bodies lying on the ash heaps.
n2500421: tn The noun вАП„Ґ÷≤„Х÷є„ЯвАО ( њavon) has a basic, twofold range of meaning: (1) basic meaning: вАЬiniquity, sin,вАЭ and (2) metonymical cause-for-effect meaning: вАЬpunishment for iniquity.вАЭ
n2500423: tn Heb вАЬthe sin of.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ч÷Ј„Ш÷Љ÷Є„Р„™вАО (khatta Њt) often means вАЬsin, rebellion,вАЭ but here it probably functions in a metonymical (cause for effect) sense: вАЬpunishment for sinвАЭ (e.g., Zech 14:19). The context focuses on the severity of the punishment of Jerusalem rather than the depths of its degradation and depravity that led to the judgment.
n2500424: tn Heb вАЬwithout a hand turned.вАЭ The preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) after the verb вАП„Ч„Х÷Љ„ЬвАО (khul) in Hos 11:6 is adversative: вАЬthe sword will turn against [AssyriaвАЩs] cities.вАЭ Other contexts with вАП„Ч„Х÷Љ„ЬвАО (khul) plus вАП„СвАО (bet) are not comparable (вАП„СвАО [bet] often being locative). However, it is not certain that hands must be adversarial, as the sword clearly is in Hos 11:6. The present translation pictures the suddenness of SodomвАЩs overthrow as an easier fate than the protracted military campaign and subsequent exile and poverty of JudahвАЩs survivors.
n2500425: tn Heb вАЬNaziritesвАЭ (so KJV). The Nazirites were consecrated under a vow to refrain from wine, contact with the dead, and cutting their hair. In Gen 49:26 and Deut 33:16, Joseph, who was not a Nazirite, is called the вАЬNazirвАЭ of his brothers. From context, many translate this as вАЬprinceвАЭ (e.g., NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT), though the nuance is uncertain. If it is valid, then princes might be understood in this context as well.
n2500426: tn The noun вАП„Т÷Љ÷і„Ц÷∞„®÷Є„ФвАО (gizrah) is used primarily in Ezekiel 41-42 (7 of its 9 uses), where it refers to a separated area of the temple complex described in EzekielвАЩs vision. It is not used of people other than here. Probably based on the reference to a precious stone, BDB 160 s.v. 1 postulated that it refers to the cutting or polishing of precious stones, but this is conjecture. The English versions handle this variously. D. R. Hillers suggests beards, hair, or eyebrows, relying on other ancient Near Eastern comparisons between lapis lazuli and the body (Lamentations [AB], 81).
n2500427: sn Lapis lazuli is a dark-blue semiprecious stone.
n2500428: tn Heb вАЬtheir outlineвАЭ or вАЬtheir form.вАЭ The Hebrew noun вАП„™÷Љ÷є„Р÷Ј„®вАО (to Њar, вАЬoutline, formвАЭ) is related to the Phoenician noun вАП„™÷Љ÷є„Р÷Ј„®вАО (to Њar, вАЬsomething gazed atвАЭ) and the Aramaic verb вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„Р÷Ј„®вАО (ta Њar, вАЬto gaze atвАЭ). It is used in reference to the forms of a woman (Gen 29:17; Deut 21:11; 1 Sam 25:3; Esth 2:7) and a man (Gen 39:11; Judg 8:18; 1 Sam 16:18; 28:14; 1 Kgs 1:6; 1 Chr 17:17; Isa 52:14; 53:2). Here it occurs in a metonymical sense: вАЬappearance.вАЭ
n2500429: tn Heb вАЬthose pierced of the sword.вАЭ The genitive-construct denotes instrumentality: вАЬthose pierced by the swordвАЭ (вАП„Ч÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ь÷µ„Щ÷Њ„Ч÷ґ„®÷ґ„СвАО, khalele kherev). The noun вАП„Ч÷Є„Ь÷Є„ЬвАО (khalal) refers to a вАЬfatal woundвАЭ and is used substantivally to refer to вАЬthe slainвАЭ (Num 19:18; 31:8, 19; 1 Sam 17:52; 2 Sam 23:8, 18; 1 Chr 11:11, 20; Isa 22:2; 66:16; Jer 14:18; 25:33; 51:49; Lam 4:9; Ezek 6:7; 30:11; 31:17, 18; 32:20; Zeph 2:12).
n2500430: tn Heb вАЬthose slain of hunger.вАЭ The genitive-construct denotes instrumentality: вАЬthose slain by hunger,вАЭ that is, those who are dying of hunger.
n2500431: tn Heb вАЬwhoвА¶вАЭ The antecedent of the relative pronoun вАП„©„Б÷ґ„Ф÷µ„ЭвАО (shehem, вАЬwhoвАЭ) are those dying of hunger in the previous line: вАП„Ю÷µ„Ч÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ь÷µ„Щ „®÷Є„Ґ÷Є„СвАО (mekhalele ra њav, вАЬthose slain of hungerвАЭ).
n2500432: tn Heb вАЬthey flow away.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ц„Х÷Љ„СвАО (zuv, вАЬto flow, gushвАЭ) is used figuratively here, meaning вАЬto pine awayвАЭ or вАЬto waste awayвАЭ from hunger. See also the next note.
n2500433: tn Heb вАЬpierced through and through.вАЭ The term вАП„Ю÷∞„У÷ї„І÷Љ÷Є„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (meduqqarim), Pual participle masculine plural from вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„І÷Ј„®вАО (daqar, вАЬto pierceвАЭ), is used figuratively. The verb вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„І÷Ј„®вАО (daqar, вАЬto pierceвАЭ) usually refers to a fatal wound inflicted by a sword or spear (Num 25:8; Judg 9:54; 1 Sam 31:4; 1 Chr 10:4; Isa 13:15; Jer 37:10; 51:4; Zech 12:10; 13:3). Here, it describes people dying from hunger. This is an example of hypocatastasis: an implied comparison between warriors being fatally pierced by sword and spear and the piercing pangs of hunger and starvation. Alternatively, one could translate, вАЬthose who hemorrhage (вАП„Ц„Х÷Љ„СвАО [zuv, вАЬflow, gushвАЭ]) [are better off] than those pierced by lack of food,вАЭ in parallel to the structure of the first line.
n2500434: tn The preposition вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min, вАЬfromвАЭ) denotes deprivation: вАЬfrom lack ofвАЭ something (BDB 580 s.v. 2.f; HALOT 598 s.v. 6).
n2500437: tn Heb вАЬeating.вАЭ The infinitive construct (from I вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО, barah) is translated as a noun. Three passages employ the verb (2 Sam 3:35; 12:17; 13:5, 6, 10) for eating when one is ill or in mourning.
n2500439: tn Heb вАЬin the destruction of the daughter of my people.вАЭ
n2500440: tn Heb вАЬhas completed.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„Ь÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (killah), Piel perfect third person masculine singular from вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (kalah, вАЬto completeвАЭ), has a range of closely related meanings: (1) вАЬto complete, bring to an end,вАЭ (2) вАЬto accomplish, finish, cease,вАЭ (3) вАЬto use up, exhaust, consume.вАЭ Used in reference to GodвАЩs wrath, it describes God unleashing his full measure of anger so that divine justice is satisfied. This is handled admirably by several English versions: вАЬThe Lord has given full vent to his wrathвАЭ (NIV), вАЬThe Lord gave full vent to his wrathвАЭ (RSV, NRSV), вАЬThe Lord vented all his furyвАЭ (NJPS), and вАЬThe Lord turned loose the full force of his furyвАЭ (TEV). Others miss the mark: вАЬThe Lord has accomplished his wrath/furyвАЭ (KJV, NKJV, ASV, NASB).
n2500442: tn The term вАП„Щ÷∞„°„Х÷є„УвАО (yesod, вАЬfoundationвАЭ) refers to the ground-level and below ground-level foundation stones of a city wall (Ps 137:7; Lam 4:11; Mic 1:6).
n2500444: tn Heb вАЬthey did not believe that.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ф÷ґ„Р÷±„Ю÷і„Щ„†„Х÷ЉвАО (he Њeminu), Hiphil perfect third person common plural from вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЯвАО ( Њaman, вАЬto believeвАЭ), ordinarily is a term of faith and trust, but occasionally it functions cognitively: вАЬto think thatвАЭ (Job 9:16; 15:22; Ps 116:10; Lam 4:12) and вАЬto be convinced thatвАЭ (Ps 27:13) (HALOT 64 s.v. I вАП„Р„Ю„ЯвАО hif.1). The semantic relationship between вАЬto believeвАЭ = вАЬto thinkвАЭ is metonymical, that is, effect for cause.
n2500445: sn The expression вАЬto enter the gatesвАЭ of a city is an idiom referring to the military conquest of that city. Ancient Near-Eastern fortified cities typically featured double and sometimes triple city gatesвАФthe bulwark of the defense of the city. Because fortified cities were enclosed with protective walls, the Achilles tendon of every city was the city gatesвАФthe weak point in the defense and the perennial point of attack by enemies (e.g., Judg 5:8, 11; 1 Sam 17:52; Isa 29:6; Jer 17:27; 51:54; Ezek 21:20, 27; Mic 1:9, 12; Neh 1:3; 2:3, 13, 17).
n2500446: tn These words do not appear in the Hebrew but are supplied to make sense of the line. The introductory causal preposition вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min) (вАЬbecauseвАЭ) indicates that this phraseвАФor something like itвАФis implied through elision.
n2500447: tn There is no main verb in the verse; it is an extended prepositional phrase. One must either assume a verbal idea such as, вАЬBut it happened due toвА¶,вАЭ or connect the verse to the following verses, which themselves are quite difficult. The former option was employed in the present translation.
n2500448: tn вАЬTheyвАЭ are apparently the people, rather than the prophets and priests mentioned in the preceding verse.
n2500449: tc The Hebrew word вАП„Ґ÷і„Х÷∞„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( њivrim) appears to be an adjective based on the root I вАП„Ґ÷і„Х÷Љ÷µ„®вАО ( њivver, вАЬblindвАЭ). The LXX, using a rare perfect optative of бЉРќ≥ќµќѓѕБѕЙ (egeir≈Н), seems to have read a form of II вАП„Ґ„Х÷Љ„®вАО ( њur, вАЬto riseвАЭ), while the Syriac reads вАЬher nobles,вАЭ possibly from reading вАП„©„В÷Є„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (sarim). The evidence is unclear.
n2500450:
tn Heb вАЬdefiled with blood.вАЭ Cf. Isa 59:3.
sn Tremper Longman (Jeremiah, Lamentations [New International Biblical Commentary], 384) notes that the priests are unclean by the blood on their garments, but blood from wounds did not make a person unclean. Murder made a person guilty but not ceremonially unclean. Jeremiah chose the vocabulary of ceremonial defilement to stress the wrongness of what they did.
n2500451: tn The grammar is uncommon. The MT has the preposition вАП„СвАО (bet, вАЬin,вАЭ вАЬby,вАЭ вАЬwith,вАЭ вАЬwhen,вАЭ etc.), the negative particle вАП„Ь÷є„РвАО (lo Њ), and then a finite verb from вАП„Щ÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЬвАО (yakhal, Qal imperfect third person masculine plural): вАЬin not they are able.вАЭ Normally вАП„Щ÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЬвАО (yakhal) would be followed by an infinitive, identifying what someone is or is not able to do, or by some other modifying clause. вАП„Ь÷є„Р „Щ÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЬвАО (lo Њ yakhal) on its own may mean вАЬthey do not prevail.вАЭ The preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) suggests possible dependence on another verb (cf. Jer 2:11, the only other verse with the sequence вАП„СвАО [bet] plus вАП„Ь÷є„РвАО [lo Њ] plus finite verb). The following verb вАП„†÷Є„Т÷Ј„ҐвАО (naga њ, вАЬtouchвАЭ) regularly indicates its object with the preposition вАП„СвАО (bet), but the preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) is already used with вАЬtheir garments.вАЭ If both are the object of вАП„†÷Є„Т÷Ј„ҐвАО (naga њ), the line would oddly read: вАЬthey touched what they could not, their garments.вАЭ The preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) can also introduce temporal clauses, though there are no examples with вАП„Ь÷є„РвАО (lo Њ) plus a finite verb. BDB 89 s.v. вАП„С÷Љ÷∞вАО III 1.b states that вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷є„РвАО can mean вАЬwithout.вАЭ BDB 407 s.v. вАП„Щ÷Є„Ы÷є„ЬвАО Qal 1.e says that the sequence вАЬthey are unable, they touchвАЭ equals вАЬthey are unable to touch.вАЭ In Jer 49:10 the meaning of вАП„Щ÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЬвАО (yakhal) is completed by a finite verb (though it is not governed by the preposition вАП„СвАО [bet]). If so here, then we may understand: вАЬwithout people being able (вАП„Щ÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЬвАО, yakhal) to touch their garments.вАЭ See GKC, 120g. This gives the picture of blind people stumbling about while others cannot help because they are afraid to touch them due to possible defilement themselves.
n2500453: tn Heb вАЬthe face of the Lord.вАЭ The term вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„†÷ґ„ФвАО (paneh, вАЬfaceвАЭ) is a synecdoche of a part (= face) for the whole person (= the Lord himself). The phrase is often translated вАЬthe presence of the Lord.вАЭ The term вАЬfaceвАЭ also functions anthropomorphically, depicting the invisible, spiritual God as though he had a physical face.
n2500454:
tc The MT reads a plural verb вАП„Ь÷є„Р „†÷Є„©„В÷Є„Р„Х÷ЉвАО (lo Њ nasa Њu, вАЬthey did not lift upвАЭ) from вАП„†÷Є„©„В÷Є„РвАО (nasa Њ, вАЬto lift upвАЭ); however, the ancient versions (LXX, Aramaic Targum, Latin Vulgate, Syriac Peshitta) have singular verbs, reflecting вАП„Ь÷є„Р „†÷Є„©„В÷Є„РвАО (lo Њ nasa Њ, вАЬhe did not lift upвАЭ). D. R. Hillers suggests that the MT plural is an intentional scribe change, to avoid the appearance that God brought about evil on the priests and elders. It may also be that the third person plural presumes an indefinite subject and the construction is used in place of a passive, but still essentially means, вАЬthe priests were not honoredвАЭ (see following note regarding the idiom). Another alternative would be to revocalize the verb as the rare Qal passive, which would yield the same result.
tn Heb вАЬdid not lift up.вАЭ The verb вАП„†÷Є„©„В÷Є„РвАО (nasa Њ) means вАЬto lift upвАЭ (the face); however, the specific contextual nuance here is probably вАЬto show considerationвАЭ (e.g., Deut 28:50; Lam 4:16) (BDB 670 s.v. 1.b.3).
n2500455:
tc The MT reads a plural verb вАП„Ь÷є„Р „Ч÷Є„†÷Є„†„Х÷ЉвАО (lo Њ khananu, вАЬthey did not show favorвАЭ) from вАП„Ч÷Є„†÷Ј„ЯвАО (khanan, вАЬto show favor, be mercifulвАЭ); however, the ancient versions (LXX, Aramaic Targum, Latin Vulgate, Syriac Peshitta) have singular verbs, reflecting вАП„Ь÷є„Р „Ч÷Є„†÷Ј„ЯвАО (lo Њ khanan, вАЬhe did not show favorвАЭ). D. R. Hillers suggests that the MT plural is an intentional scribal change to avoid the appearance that God brought about evil on the priests and elders. It may also be that the third person plural presumes an indefinite subject and the construction is used in place of a passive, but still essentially means вАЬthe elders were not shown mercy.вАЭ Another alternative would be to revocalize the verb as the rare Qal passive, which would yield the same result.
tn The basic meaning of the verb вАП„Ч÷Є„†÷Ј„ЯвАО (khanan) is вАЬto show favor [to], be gracious [to].вАЭ In some contexts this can mean вАЬto spareвАЭ the lives of someone (Deut 7:2; 28:50; Job 19:21; Lam 4:16) (BDB 336 s.v. 1.c), though it is not clear whether that is the case here.
n2500456: tn Heb вАЬOur eyes failed in vain for help.вАЭ
n2500457: tn Heb вАЬtheyвАЭ; this has been specified in the translation as вАЬour enemiesвАЭ for clarity.
n2500460: tn The bird referred to here could be one of several species of eagles but more likely is the griffin-vulture (cf. NEB вАЬvulturesвАЭ). However, because eagles are more commonly associated with swiftness than vultures in contemporary English, вАЬeaglesвАЭ was used in the translation.
n2500461: tn Or вАЬin the heavens.вАЭ The Hebrew term вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (shamayim) may be translated вАЬheaven(s)вАЭ or вАЬsky,вАЭ depending on the context.
n2500462: tn Heb вАЬthe anointed one of the Lord.вАЭ The term вАЬkingвАЭ is added in the translation to clarify the referent of the phrase вАЬthe LordвАЩs anointed.вАЭ
n2500465: tn Heb вАЬunder his shadow.вАЭ The term вАП„¶÷µ„ЬвАО (tsel, вАЬshadowвАЭ) is used figuratively here to refer the source of protection from military enemies. In the same way that the shade of a tree gives physical relief and protection from the heat of the sun (e.g., Judg 9:15; Job 40:22; Ps 80:11; Song 2:3; Ezek 17:23; 31:6, 12, 17; Hos 4:13; 14:8; Jon 4:5, 6), a faithful and powerful king can provide вАЬshadeвАЭ (= protection) from enemies and military attack (Num 14:19; Ps 91:1; Isa 30:2, 3; 49:2; 51:16; Jer 48:45; Lam 4:20).
n2500466: tn The phrase вАЬfor nowвАЭ is added in the translation to highlight the implied contrast between the present joy of the Gentiles (4:21a) and their future judgment (4:21b).
n2500468: tn Heb вАЬthe cup.вАЭ Judgment is often depicted as a cup of wine that God forces a person to drink, causing him to lose consciousness, with red wine drooling out of his mouth. He resembles corpses lying on the ground as a result of the actual onslaught of the LordвАЩs judgment. The drunkard, reeling and staggering, causing bodily injury to himself, is an apt metaphor to describe the devastating effects of GodвАЩs judgment. Just as a cup of poison kills all those who are forced to drink it, the cup of GodвАЩs wrath destroys all those who must drink it (e.g., Ps 75:9; Isa 51:17, 22; Jer 25:15, 17, 28; 49:12; 51:7; Lam 4:21; Ezek 23:33; Hab 2:16).
n2500469: tn The imperfect verb вАЬwill passвАЭ may also be a jussive, continuing the element of request: вАЬlet the cup passвА¶вАЭ
n2500471: tn Heb вАЬyour iniquity.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ґ÷Є„Х÷є„ЯвАО ( њavon) has a broad range of meanings, including: (1) iniquity, (2) guilt of iniquity, and (3) consequence or punishment for iniquity (cause-effect metonymical relation). The context suggests that вАЬpunishment for sinвАЭ is most appropriate here (e.g., Gen 4:13; 19:15; Exod 28:38, 43; Lev 5:1, 17; 7:18; 10:17; 16:22; 17:16; 19:8; 20:17, 19; 22:16; 26:39, 41, 43; Num 5:31; 14:34; 18:1, 23; 30:15; 1 Sam 25:24; 28:10; 2 Sam 14:9; 2 Kgs 7:9; Job 10:14; Pss 31:11; 69:28; 106:43; Prov 5:22; Isa 5:18; 30:13; 40:2; 53:6, 11; 64:5, 6; Jer 51:6; Lam 4:22; 5:7; Ezek 4:4-6, 17; 7:16; 14:10; 18:19-20; 21:30, 34 HT [21:25, 29 ET]; 24:23; 32:27; 35:5; 39:23; 44:10, 12).
n2500472: tn Heb вАЬwill be completed.вАЭ The verb вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„ЭвАО (tam) is Qal perfect from вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЭвАО (tamam, вАЬto be completeвАЭ). The translation understands it as an example of the so-called вАЬprophetic perfect,вАЭ describing a future event viewed as вАЬcomplete.вАЭ Some would call this вАЬas good as done,вАЭ or certain to take place from the viewpoint of the prophet. It has also been viewed as a simple perfect: вАЬyour punishment is ended.вАЭ
n2500474: tn The noun вАП„Ґ÷Є„Х÷є„ЯвАО ( њavon) is repeated twice in this verse, denoting first вАЬpunishment for iniquityвАЭ (v. 22a), and then вАЬiniquityвАЭ (v. 22b). See a preceding translatorвАЩs note on the broad range of meanings for this word. The repetition of the same root with different meanings creates an ironic polysemantic wordplay: ZionвАЩs вАЬpunishmentвАЭ for its sin is about to come to an end, but the punishment for EdomвАЩs вАЬsinвАЭ is about to begin.
n250051: sn The speaking voice is now that of a choir singing the communityвАЩs lament in the first person plural. The poem is not an alphabetic acrostic like the preceding chapters but has 22 verses, the same as the number of letters in the Hebrew alphabet.
n250052: tn The basic meaning of вАП„Ц÷Є„Ы÷Ј„®вАО (zakhar) is вАЬto remember, call to mindвАЭ (HALOT 270 s.v. I вАП„Ц„Ы„®вАО). Although often used of recollection of past events, вАП„Ц÷Є„Ы÷Ј„®вАО (zakhar, вАЬto rememberвАЭ) can also describe consideration of present situations: вАЬto consider, think aboutвАЭ something present (BDB 270 s.v. 5), hence вАЬreflect on,вАЭ the most appropriate nuance here. Verses 1-6 describe the present plight of Jerusalem. The parallel requests вАП„Ф÷Ј„С÷Љ÷µ„Щ„Ш „Х÷Љ„®÷∞„Р÷µ„ФвАО (habbet ure Њeh, вАЬLook and see!вАЭ) have a present-time orientation as well. See also 2:1 and 3:19-20.
n250053: tn Heb вАЬLook!вАЭ Although often used in reference to visual perception, вАП„†÷Є„С÷Ј„ШвАО (navat, вАЬto lookвАЭ) can also refer to cognitive consideration and mental attention shown to a situation: вАЬto regardвАЭ (e.g., 1 Sam 16:7; 2 Kgs 3:14), or вАЬto pay attention to, considerвАЭ (e.g., Isa 22:8; 51:1, 2).
n250054: tn Although normally occurring in reference to visual sight, вАП„®÷Є„Р÷Є„ФвАО (ra Њah) is often used in reference to cognitive processes and mental observation. See the note on вАЬConsiderвАЭ at 2:20.
n250055: tn Heb вАЬOur inheritanceвАЭ or вАЬOur inherited possessions/property.вАЭ The term вАП„†÷Ј„Ч÷≤„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (nakhalah) has a range of meanings: (1) вАЬinheritance,вАЭ (2) вАЬportion, shareвАЭ and (3) вАЬpossession, property.вАЭ The land of Canaan was given by the Lord to Israel as its inheritance (Deut 4:21; 15:4; 19:10; 20:16; 21:23; 24:4; 25:19; 26:1; Josh 20:6) and distributed among the tribes, clans, and families (Num 16:14; 36:2; Deut 29:7; Josh 11:23; 13:6; 14:3, 13; 17:4, 6, 14; 19:49; 23:4; Judg 18:1; Ezek 45:1; 47:22; 48:29). Through the land, the family provided an inheritance (property) to its children, with the firstborn receiving pride of position (Gen 31:14; Num 27:7-11; 36:3, 8; 1 Kgs 21:3, 4; Job 42:15; Prov 19:14; Ezek 46:16). Here the parallelism between вАЬour inheritanceвАЭ and вАЬour homesвАЭ would allow for the specific referent of the phrase вАЬour inheritanceвАЭ to be (1) land or (2) material possessions, or given the nature of the poetry in Lamentations, to carry both meanings at the same time.
n250056: tn Heb вАЬour homes [are turned over] to foreigners.вАЭ
n250057: tn Heb вАЬsilver.вАЭ The term вАЬsilverвАЭ is a synecdoche of the particular (= silver) for the general (= money).
n250058: tn Heb вАЬWe drink our water for silver.вАЭ
n250059: tn Heb вАЬour wood comes for a price.вАЭ
n2500510: tn Heb вАЬWe are hard-driven on our necks.вАЭ
n2500511: sn For the theological allusion that goes beyond physical rest, see, e.g., Deut 12:10; 25:19; Josh 1:13; 11:23; 2 Sam 7:1, 11; 1 Chron 22:18; 2 Chron 14:6-7.
n2500512: tn Heb вАЬwe have given the handвАЭ; cf. NRSV вАЬWe have made a pact.вАЭ This is a Semitic idiom meaning вАЬto make a treaty withвАЭ someone, placing oneself in a subservient position as vassal. The prophets criticized these treaties.
n2500513: tn Heb вАЬbread.вАЭ The term вАЬbreadвАЭ is a synecdoche of the specific (= bread) for the general (= food).
n2500514: tn Heb вАЬfathers,вАЭ but here the term also refers to вАЬforefathers,вАЭ i.e., more distant ancestors.
n2500516: tc The Kethib is written вАП„Р÷≤„†÷Ј„Ч÷∞„†„Х÷ЉвАО ( Њanakhnu, вАЬweвАЭ), but the Qere reads вАП„Х÷Ј„Р÷≤„†÷Ј„Ч÷∞„†„Х÷ЉвАО (va Њanakhnu, вАЬbut weвАЭ). The Qere is supported by many medieval Hebrew mss, as well as most of the ancient versions (Aramaic Targum, Syriac Peshitta, Latin Vulgate). The вАП„ХвАО (vav) prefixed to вАП„Х÷Ј„Р÷≤„†÷Ј„Ч÷∞„†„Х÷ЉвАО (va Њanakhnu) functions either in a disjunctive sense (вАЬbutвАЭ) or resultant sense (вАЬsoвАЭ).

tnThe Hebrew verb вАЬforgetвАЭ often means вАЬto not pay attention to, ignore,вАЭ just as the Hebrew вАЬrememberвАЭ often means вАЬto consider, attend to.вАЭ
sn The verbs вАЬto forgetвАЭ and вАЬto rememberвАЭ are often used figuratively in scripture when God is the subject, particularly in contexts of judgment (God forgets his people) and restoration of blessing (God remembers his people). In this case, the verb вАЬto forgetвАЭ functions as a hypocatastasis (implied comparison), drawing a comparison between GodвАЩs judgment and rejection of Jerusalem to a person forgetting that Jerusalem even exists. GodвАЩs judgment of Jerusalem was so intense and enduring that it seemed as though he had forgotten her. The synonymous parallelism makes this clear.

n2500539: tn The compound conjunction вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„Щ „Р÷і„ЭвАО (ki  Њim) functions to limit the preceding clause: вАЬunless, orвА¶вАЭ (e.g., Ruth 3:18; Isa 65:6; Amos 3:7) (BDB 474 s.v. 2.a): вАЬBring us back to yourselfвА¶unless you have utterly rejected usвАЭ (as in the present translation), or вАЬBring us back to yourselfвА¶Or have you utterly rejected us?вАЭ It is JeremiahвАЩs plea that the Lord be willing to relent of his anger and restore a repentant nation to himself.
